pages.title: PDF Relevance Filter — Live Demo — COGS 160
pages.excerpt: PDF Relevance Filter — Live Demo — COGS 160 COGS 160 · Live Demo · The 7-Step Method in Action PDF Relevance Filter Given a K-ATLAS question and a submitted paper, determine whether the paper provides evidence relevant to that question. This is the foundation of quality control for the entire corpus. THE PROBLEM Knowledge Atlas has a question bank of 130 research questions spanning 34 topics — from Attention Restoration Theory to Fractal Patterns. Students and automated pipelines submit PDFs as evidence for these questions. But how do you know a paper is actually relevant? A paper about “attention deficit hyperactivity disorder in natural classroom settings” mentions both “attention” and “natural,” but it is about clinical ADHD, not attention restoration from nature exposure. A paper about forest bathing and cortisol is adjacent to an attention restoration question — same environment, wrong outcome measure. If irrelevant papers enter the corpus, every downstream analysis is contaminated. We need a filter that takes a K-ATLAS question and a submitted PDF and determines: does this paper provide evidence relevant to this specific question? The filter must understand what “relevant” means in the context of each question’s evidence type, population, and environmental variable. This is the foundation of quality control for the entire system. STEP 1 Convene Panel Panel Prompt You are running a design panel for a PDF relevance filter. The panelists are: - Susan Haack (epistemology, f

Go to Contribute Page → 1 Overview — What You Are Doing and Why It Matters K-ATLAS — the Knowledge Architecture for Translating Life-science and Affective-environment Science — is a living knowledge base for COGS 160. It is not a pre-built database. It is built by you, this quarter, from primary literature. Every paper you collect becomes part of the corpus that future students, TAs, and the ATLAS reasoning engine draw on when answering questions about how built environments affect human cognition, affect, and wellbeing. This assignment is universal: every student in the class completes it in Week 1 . There is no long research phase, no elaborate method selection, no coding. The task is straightforward: find good empirical papers, upload them with proper citations, and let the system classify and store them. pages.excerpt: Writing Google AI Citation Queries — K-ATLAS · COGS 160 K- ATLAS UC San Diego · Cognitive Science Home A0 Assignment Setup Schedule On This Page Why Google AI Citation? Core Method How AI Citation works Query anatomy The five components Writing Queries Boolean vs. AI Citation Sentence patterns Evidential framing Advanced: mechanism queries Practice Worked examples Common mistakes From K-ATLAS question bank Submission Search workflow Evaluating results CSV format Pre-submit checklist K-ATLAS Student Module Writing Google AI Citation Queries A complete guide to formulating natural-language search queries that retrieve peer-reviewed evidence about environment–behavior relationships — the foundation of your K-ATLAS article submission. Why Google AI Citation? Academic search has changed fundamentally. Traditional Boolean keyword search — stringing together terms with AND, OR, and quotation marks — was designed for index-based retrieval systems that matched exact strings. Google AI Citation (the AI Overview with source citations that appears at the top of Google results) works differently: it reads your query as a natural-language question, reasons about what you are asking, and returns papers whose arguments and findings answer that question, even when the papers use different terminology than you do. For environmental psychology research — a field where the same phenomenon might be called "restorative experience," "psychological restoration," "cognitive recovery," or "directed at

pages.excerpt: T4.a — Heuristic audit — Knowledge Atlas Track 4 · UX research · Deliverable T4.a Heuristic audit of the K-Atlas public site (10 findings) Span Weeks 3–4 Hardness Medium Points 12 / 75 Method Nielsen (1994) ten heuristics Full rubric → Track 4 deliverables T4.a T4.b T4.b.2 T4.c T4.d T4.e T4.f T4.g ◎ Goal what this deliverable teaches You will produce ten structured findings against the K-Atlas public site, each one coded to one of Nielsen's ten usability heuristics. By the end you will be able to tell a design-decision story in the language any working UX team uses: heuristic · scenario · observed · expected · severity · recommendation . This deliverable is medium-hardness because the art is not in finding problems — the current site has many — but in writing findings that another student could independently reproduce without seeing your screenshots. A finding that only you can interpret scores band 1; a finding that reproduces in someone else's browser scores band 3. › What you'll do the steps Read Nielsen's ten heuristics. Keep the list open in a tab; you will cite each finding against exactly one heuristic. Walk the eight pages listed in the Files and links section below. Spend 5–10 minutes on each page with a task in mind (the page-specific task is suggested inline). When you notice friction, stop and record a finding in the schema below. Do not try to fix anything on the live site. Take a screenshot, note what you expected vs. what happened. Produce ten findings total, c

pages.title: T4.b.2 — Topic-page variant evaluation — Knowledge Atlas
headings: ☷ The six variants to walk through
pages.excerpt: T4.b.2 — Topic-page variant evaluation — Knowledge Atlas Track 4 · UX research · Deliverable T4.b.2 Topic-page variant evaluation (comparative usability study) Span Week 4 Hardness Medium-hard Points 10 / 75 Method Nielsen heuristics + Rosson & Carroll scenarios Full rubric → Track 4 deliverables T4.a T4.b T4.b.2 T4.c T4.d T4.e T4.f T4.g ◎ Goal The Knowledge Atlas ships six topic-page variants — the same underlying crosswalk data rendered as six different interaction patterns. You will walk each of your three T4.b archetypes through each of the six variants (15 walkthroughs), record timings and friction per walkthrough, and produce a defended design recommendation for which variant should become the site's default topic page. This is a real evaluation whose output will inform a production decision. The design recommendation in your comparison report goes straight to the instructor's short-list for the default ka_topics.html entry. › What you'll do Open your three T4.b archetype files. If the scenarios are not sharp, sharpen them first (a vague scenario produces vague walkthroughs). For each of the 15 cells (3 archetypes × 5 new variants, plus 3 × 1 Classic View = 15 total when you count the Classic View separately; we count the six variants), open the variant in a fresh browser tab, execute the archetype's primary scenario, and record what happens. Use the walkthrough-matrix form below to capture every cell. Measure click count and rough time per scenario with a stopwatch. Do

pages.title: T4.b — Scenario-based walkthroughs — Knowledge Atlas
headings: ☷ The three archetypes & their canonical profiles
pages.excerpt: T4.b — Scenario-based walkthroughs — Knowledge Atlas Track 4 · UX research · Deliverable T4.b Scenario-based walkthroughs on 3 archetype roles Span Week 4 Hardness Medium Points 5 / 75 Method Rosson & Carroll scenario-based design Full rubric → Track 4 deliverables T4.a T4.b T4.b.2 T4.c T4.d T4.e T4.f T4.g ◎ Goal For each of three user archetypes, write one concrete scenario the archetype is likely to bring to the site. A scenario is a short, literal, testable description of a user's task — not a list of features they might want. The three scenarios become the evaluation instrument for T4.b.2 and for the T4.c usability pilot. This deliverable is worth five points because its work product is small but load-bearing: every later Track 4 deliverable depends on the scenarios being sharp. A vague scenario ("a user wants to learn about lighting") cannot be walked through; a sharp scenario ("Aisha, a COGS 160 Track 1 student, wants to find the five topic bundles with the highest paper count that involve luminous , because she is picking the framework to anchor her tagging schema against") can. › What you'll do Read the three archetype profiles below. Each links to the canonical persona section in the Knowledge Atlas persona doc. For each archetype, write one primary scenario and one alternate scenario (the archetype's second-most-likely task). A scenario must: name the user concretely, name the goal in terms the user would recognise, state what "success" looks like, and be executable

pages.excerpt: T4.c — Moderated usability pilot — Knowledge Atlas Track 4 · UX research · Deliverable T4.c Moderated usability pilot (n = 5) Span Weeks 5–6 Hardness Hard Points 15 / 75 Method Think-aloud protocol (Nielsen & Landauer 1993; Ericsson & Simon 1993) Full rubric → Track 4 deliverables T4.a T4.b T4.b.2 T4.c T4.d T4.e T4.f T4.g ◎ Goal what this deliverable teaches You will run a short moderated usability study with five participants drawn from at least two of the three user archetypes (public visitor, COGS 160 student, administrator). Each participant works through three scripted scenarios from T4.b while thinking aloud. You will record what actually happened, time each task, code moments of friction, and produce a structured findings file that feeds directly into T4.d (severity triage) and T4.f (redesign proposal). This is the hardest deliverable in Track 4 because the craft is not in running the sessions — the protocol is short — but in resisting the temptation to explain, defend, or rescue the interface while a participant is stuck. The Nielsen & Landauer (1993) result that five users surface roughly 85% of usability issues assumes an observer who reports what was observed rather than what the observer wishes had been observed. › What you'll do the steps Recruit five participants across at least two archetypes. Friends and classmates are fine; do not recruit anyone who has already audited the site for you (that contaminates the pilot). Write a one-page moderator script covering

pages.excerpt: T4.d — Severity rubric and backlog — Knowledge Atlas Track 4 · UX research · Deliverable T4.d Severity rubric & prioritised backlog Span Week 6 Hardness Medium Points 5 / 75 Method Nielsen severity (1995) + RICE prioritisation Full rubric → Track 4 deliverables T4.a T4.b T4.b.2 T4.c T4.d T4.e T4.f T4.g ◎ Goal what this deliverable teaches You will take the union of findings from T4.a (heuristic audit) and T4.c (usability pilot), deduplicate them, rate each on Nielsen's five-level severity scale, and emit a ranked backlog using a RICE-style score (Reach · Impact · Confidence · Effort). The output is a single backlog.csv that the T4.f redesign deliverable consumes as input: the top three rows drive three of your proposed redesigns. The craft here is defending severity ratings with evidence. A naive backlog rates everything 3/5 because 3 is the middle; a band-3 backlog spans at least four severity levels and names, for each rating, the exact pilot quote or audit observation that justifies it. › What you'll do the steps Merge your T4.a findings (10 rows) and T4.c recurring findings (typically 5–12 rows) into a single CSV. Deduplicate exact matches; near-duplicates become one row with both sources cited. For each finding, fill five fields: severity (1–5), reach (how many of the three archetypes hit this), impact (1–3; task-failure = 3), confidence (1–3; "seen in pilot twice" = 3, "plausible from audit only" = 1), effort (S / M / L — your estimate of redesign work). Compute a RICE

pages.excerpt: T4.e — Reproducibility check — Knowledge Atlas Track 4 · UX research · Deliverable T4.e Reproducibility check: paired-replication of UX findings Span Week 7 Hardness Hard Points 13 / 75 Method Direct-replication design (after Brandt et al. 2014) Full rubric → Track 4 deliverables T4.a T4.b T4.b.2 T4.c T4.d T4.e T4.f T4.g ◎ Goal what this deliverable teaches You will pair with a classmate and each attempt to reproduce five of the other person's findings — without access to their screenshots or commentary, working only from the finding schema (heuristic, scenario, page, expected vs. observed). Each reproduction produces a binary judgement (reproduces / does not reproduce / partial) and a short narrative note. The deliverable is a replication_report.md that commits to a reproducibility rate and reflects on which pieces of the finding schema were most load-bearing. Usability findings have the same epistemic status as behavioural experiment results: a finding that reproduces on a second observer's machine is evidence; one that does not is either poorly specified or poorly grounded. This deliverable is hard because it forces you to audit your own T4.a/T4.c schema quality through a colleague's failure to reproduce it, which is less comfortable than writing new findings. › What you'll do the steps Pair up. Your instructor will assign pairs by track-cohort; you can swap if both parties agree. Exchange five findings each (ten reproductions per student total). Send only the schema field

pages.excerpt: T4.f — Redesign proposal — Knowledge Atlas Track 4 · UX research · Deliverable T4.f Redesign proposal: three mid-fidelity designs with pre-registered metrics Span Weeks 7–8 Hardness Medium Points 10 / 75 Method Sketch → critique → revise (Buxton 2007); Goals-Operators-Methods-Selection analysis (Card, Moran & Newell 1983) Full rubric → Track 4 deliverables T4.a T4.b T4.b.2 T4.c T4.d T4.e T4.f T4.g ◎ Goal what this deliverable teaches You will take the top three findings from your T4.d backlog — filtered through T4.e to keep only those that reproduced — and produce a mid-fidelity redesign for each. A mid-fidelity redesign is more committal than a sketch but less committal than working code: a wireframe or annotated mockup that names every new affordance, explains every changed label, and declares a pre-registered success metric that a future usability pilot could measure. This is a medium-hardness deliverable because the temptation is to let yourself off the hook by producing a vague "looks better" mockup. Three disciplines prevent this. First, each redesign must be tied by ID to a backlog row, so the problem it solves is non-negotiable. Second, each redesign carries a pre-registered success metric — before you have evidence it works. Third, each redesign is paired with a GOMS-style task-time estimate that an observer could falsify. › What you'll do the steps Load your T4.d backlog.csv . Filter out any rows rated NONE or UNDERSPECIFIED in T4.e. From what remains, pick the thre

pages.excerpt: T4.g — Final report — Knowledge Atlas Track 4 · UX research · Deliverable T4.g Track 4 final report Span Week 8 Hardness Easy-Medium Points 5 / 75 Method Reflective research writing (Schön 1983; Gould & Lewis 1985) Full rubric → Track 4 deliverables T4.a T4.b T4.b.2 T4.c T4.d T4.e T4.f T4.g ◎ Goal what this deliverable teaches The final report is a five-section reflective document (≈ 1,500 words) that knits T4.a through T4.f into a single narrative. It is a short deliverable because the hard evaluative work has already happened; what remains is to write it up at a level an external reader could follow, with explicit claims about what the evidence shows, what the evidence does not show, and which redesigns you would defend if an engineering team asked you to justify spending three sprints on them. This is the only deliverable in Track 4 where writing quality is graded explicitly. The department standard is Williams' clarity principles (character-action sentences, old-new flow, stress position at sentence end). We are not asking for polished prose; we are asking for prose an engineer would trust enough to act on. › What you'll do the steps Open your T4.a, T4.b, T4.b.2, T4.c, T4.d, T4.e, and T4.f output files side-by-side. Re-read each with a pen. Mark every claim that you can now defend with quantified evidence, and every claim that rests on one observation. Write Section 1 (≈ 200 words) — What we asked . Two scenarios, three archetypes, six variants. What the research question

pages.excerpt: Technical Setup — COGS 160 — Knowledge Atlas On this page 0 · Big Picture 1 · Choose AI Tool 2 · Install Software 3 · Course Repository 4 · Troubleshooting 5 · Track-Specific Setup ✓ Quick-Start Checklist Week 2 Exercises → COGS 160 · Technical Setup Guide Set Up Your Development Environment This page walks you through everything you need to start working on your track. No prior experience with AI tools, Git, or programming is assumed. Follow the steps in order — each one builds on the last. 0 The Big Picture: How This Course Works Understand the workflow before you install anything In COGS 160, you build real software tools that contribute to the Knowledge Atlas — a research system that organizes scientific evidence about how built environments affect human cognition and well-being. The key difference from a normal programming class is that you work with an AI coding assistant, not alone. The workflow is: you think , the AI writes code , and you verify and improve . You are the scientist-designer; the AI is the fast typist. Your job is to define what to build, evaluate whether it works, and fix what goes wrong. You are not expected to be a programmer. Many students in this course have never written code before. That is fine. The AI tools handle the syntax; you handle the thinking. What you need is curiosity, willingness to experiment, and the ability to describe what you want clearly. Here is what each piece of the system does: The Knowledge Atlas Website This is the public-

pages.excerpt: Thursday Exploration Session · Knowledge Atlas Knowledge Atlas · UCSD CogSci Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course Sign In Self-Guided Session · Thursday Exploring Knowledge Atlas Seven tasks designed for seven kinds of student curiosity. Work through any two or three that match what you care about, then write a short reflection. Format: Self-directed, pairs or solo Duration: ~75 minutes Deliverable: 1-page reflection (see bottom) Application: Knowledge Atlas (local) What this session is for The goal is not to complete all seven tasks. It is to find one or two topics you actually want to work on — questions where you feel genuine curiosity or frustration about the lack of evidence. ATLAS is a research tool in progress. It contains real papers, real evidence claims, and real gaps. The gaps are the interesting part. As you explore, keep a running list of things that surprise you, things that feel wrong, and claims you want to challenge. These notes are the raw material for your project proposal (due Week 4). You are also looking for places where ATLAS could be better — missing papers, weak claims, features you wish existed. If you find one, that is your contribution opportunity. 🔍 Find a Topic Identify one or two neuroarchitecture questions you want to investigate this quarter ⚡ Find a Gap Locate at least one claim that is poorly supported, contested, or missing from the evidence base ✍️ Write a Reflection One page: what you explored, what you found, what you want to

The Image Tagger produces the per-image latent-variable tags; the Tagging Contractor manages the contracts (schemas, acceptance criteria, validation sweeps) that tell the Tagger what a legitimate tag set looks like. An optional third repository, the Outcome Contractor , layers in outcome-defined search: it lets a user query the tagged image base for photographs that are predicted to produce a particular psychological outcome (say, restoration, or threat-perception). Plan for about an hour — thirty minutes to fork, clone, and install, another thirty for the GitHub-workflow walk through at the bottom of this page. Scope Two repositories required, one optional. Everything in Parts A–B refers to kirsh-lab/image_tagger and kirsh-lab/Tagging_Contractor . A third repository, kirsh-lab/Outcome_Contractor , is needed only if your sub-team is working on outcome-defined image retrieval. The Tagger writes a JSON tag set; the Contractor decides whether that tag set is admissible against a contract; the Outcome Contractor (if cloned) layers outcome-prediction queries on top of the admitted tag base. The contract between the Tagger and the Contractor is the only thing Track 1 needs to honour. Prerequisites GitHub account wit

Learn data verification and pipeline architecture. Start Track 1 Setup → Learn about Track 1 What the Image Tagger Is At its simplest, the Image Tagger is an image database that lets scientists see real-life examples of the stimuli their experiments are supposed to tell us about. You can use it to locate ecologically valid photographs of the kinds of rooms, corridors, lobbies, cafés, or outdoor spaces a hypothesis turns on, rather than relying on stylised lab renderings or hand-picked exemplars. At a deeper level, by running tagging procedures that extract what we believe are the abstract features of an environment — visual complexity, biophilic content, regional fractal dimension, shape, size, openness, material composition, and so on — we can look for commonalities among environments that look superficially very different. Equally important, we can then query images using the properties of the stimuli used in published experiments, rather than searching by everyday folk terms such as "dining room," "corridor," or "wood floors." This is what makes the tagger a

pages.excerpt: Track 2 — Article Intake · COGS 160 At las · COGS 160 160 Syllabus A0 A1 Track 1 Track 2 Track 3 Track 4 160 Student Profile ← Track 2 / Article Intake Track 2 — Article Intake This is the student intake surface for Track 2 article collection. It is PDF-first: start by uploading the papers you have already found on your assigned research front. Duplicate detection runs server-side during staging (hash, DOI, fuzzy title + authors). If you are signed in, your submissions are attributed to your student record and count toward your Track 2 metrics; if you are not signed in they are recorded anonymously — that attribution is the difference between this page and the public propose-an-article form. Assigned research front: check your Track 2 profile Minimum per week: 15 — 50 depending on phase Quality bar: experimental papers with retrievable abstracts Rules at a glance Five gates every Track 2 submission passes through Immediate gate — duplicate detection As soon as a PDF is inspected, KA computes a SHA-256 hash and cross-checks the corpus by hash, DOI, and fuzzy title + authors. If the paper is already in KA, the upload is stopped and no second copy is stored. Security gate — untrusted files Public uploads are untrusted until validated. KA verifies PDF magic bytes, rejects encrypted or malformed files, writes to a quarantine directory, and only promotes validated PDFs into the main corpus through an isolated worker. Track 2 rule — no orphan PDFs Every staged PDF must carry at leas

Your job is to make Article Eater hungry and well-fed. Use the new Track 2 roadmap first. This page remains the long-form conceptual and status brief. The primary student path now lives in track2_hub.html and the week pages. Open Track 2 roadmap → Start Week 3.5 → Open intake → What Article Finder Is At its simplest, Article Finder is the front half of the Knowledge Atlas pipeline : it searches the scientific literature for papers that might contain evidence the Atlas needs, scores them for how much they would change what we currently believe, downloads the PDFs that clear that bar, and hands them to Article Eater for structured extraction. Think of it as the part of the system that goes shopping , and Article Eater as the part that cooks . At a deeper level, Article Finder is where the Atlas's epistemic priorities meet the world's publication record. Its Value-of-Information (VOI) scoring is a deliberate bet: studies that would fill a gap in the coherentist web of evidence are worth more than studies that merely reinforce what the At

headings: You can start from an existing room model and identify which transformations it can support.
You can map a research-style request onto architectural factors such as ceiling height, wall curvature, enclosure, light, and prospect/refuge.
You can implement or specify controlled edits without breaking hierarchy, openings, materials, or scene structure.
You can build one browser-runnable A-Frame scene that preserves the logic of a real experiment rather than merely looking impressive.
You can package variants, logs, and explanations so another researcher could understand and reuse the work.
How to work with AI in this track

You will work as a small production studio rather than as isolated individual projects. With a compact team, everyone contributes across roles rather than specialising in one lane. The areas of responsibility below rotate or overlap as the quarter progresses.
pages.excerpt: Track 3 Overview · Track 3 · COGS 160 Home / COGS 160 / Track 3 / Track 3 Overview COGS 160 · Track 3 Track 3: AI and VR studio A student-facing roadmap for the final 7.5 weeks, focused on benchmark rooms, controlled transformations, and one browser-runnable A-Frame research scene. Track 3 AI + VR studio Weekly pages and deliverables Overview Week 1 Week 2 Week 3 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Week 7.5 Team Hub Setup Guide Resources Workbook Studio framing Track 3 is about controlled built-environment experimentation. You will inspect and clean room assets in Blender, learn how to alter them in experimentally meaningful ways, and package those results so they can be used in browser-runnable A-Frame scenes. This means the class is not mainly about text-to-3D generation and not mainly about Unreal engineering. It is about taking strong source models and altering them in ways that are geometrically plausible, academically legible, and easy for other students and researchers to review. Some requests will not arrive in everyday design language. A researcher may ask for a room that increases refuge while preserving visibility control, or increases awe without making the room feel cold. You must learn how to translate that language into room edits, and how to explain those edits back to a user. Track 3 Setup path One baseline, one sequence Today Setup Guide Week 1 Resources Workbook Weekly Roadmap Team Hub Official Track 3 Contract Use this rule: Track 3 students may inspect and edit assets in Blender, but the final course submission path is a browser-runnable A-Frame / WebXR scene. Unity and Unreal are optional prototype environments, not the required student baseline. Primary tools Blender for asset inspection and cleanup. A-Frame for the eventual submitted scene. Git and a browser for review and sharing. Not required now No Unreal starter repo, no headset pairing, and no large game-engine install just to begin the course. First real goal Build a strong benchmark room shortlist and prove that the rooms can support controlled architectural edits. Why The Course Uses A-Frame Advantages A-Frame scenes run in the browser, are easy to inspect, easy to submit through Git, and much easier for TAs and future students to review than a heavy engine project. Limitations A-Frame is not the best path for maximal photorealism, dense physics, or the most ambitious headset-specific interaction design. Those are real limits. pages.excerpt: Track THREE: AI & VR — COGS 160 Knowledge At las · COGS 160 160 Syllabus A0 A1 Track 1 Track 2 Track 3 Track 4 160 Student Profile On this page Overview Hybrid Workflow AI Tools Envelope & Latents Base Rooms Student Assignment Weekly Syllabus How to Set Up → COGS 160 · Track 3 of 4 Track THREE: AI & VR Experiments Learn to build controlled neuroarchitecture experiments from strong room assets. In this course you inspect and transform rooms in Blender, then deliver browser-runnable A-Frame scenes that students, TAs, and researchers can actually open, test, and critique. Start Track 3 Setup → Learn about Track 3 Track Overview Neuroarchitecture experiments need environments whose properties can be varied in a controlled way. Track 3 builds that missing layer: benchmark rooms, transformation logic, and one browser-runnable experimental scene. The aim is not to produce the most photorealistic environment possible. The aim is to produce a scene that preserves the logic of a real experiment and can be reviewed by the rest of the course without a heavy engine install. The course contract: Blender is the asset-preparation tool. A-Frame is the official submission path for final Track 3 scenes. Unity and Unreal remain acceptable prototype environments, but they are not the grading contract and not the required student baseline. pages.excerpt: Track 4 · In-class Exercise — Persona Panel Prompts Skip to main content For tomorrow's class — Thursday, 16 April 2026. Each Track 4 student takes one persona, builds a panel of three AI advisors who can role-play that persona, and walks the site through that persona's eyes. By the end of class you should have (a) a finished prompt, (b) a critique from the instructor on that prompt, and (c) the beginning of a journey-walkthrough document grounded in your persona's needs. What we are doing and why This week's deliverable for the UX track is a user journey — at the very least, a serious attempt to understand what a real user of the Knowledge Atlas is trying to do, where they get stuck, and what would help. Walking the site as yourself is not enough; you already know how it works. The discipline of taking on a persona forces you to encounter the site the way a stranger does. We will use AI panels as a leverage tool. Each of you will design a three-member panel — three AI agents, each instantiated with a slightly different version of your persona — and use the panel to generate, critique, and pressure-test journey observations. The quality of what the panel returns depends almost entirely on the quality of the prompt you write to instantiate it. Your work today is to write that prompt well. Step 1 · Pick your persona Eight personas correspond to the K-ATLAS user types and roles. Each Track 4 student claims a different persona where possible. P1 · Maya Chen (160 Student) P2 · Dr.

Analyze their information needs, improve interaction design, and measure whether the system actually works for them. Start Track 4 Setup → Learn about Track 4 Track Overview Knowledge Atlas is a working research instrument that will be used by very different kinds of people: a nineteen-year-old undergraduate meeting this literature for the first time, a postdoc hunting for a null finding, an architect with ten minutes between client meetings, a high-school teacher building a lesson plan, a science journalist looking for a defensible quote, a collaborating scientist uploading evidence, and the principal investigator watching the whole epistemic graph. Each of these users wants different things from the same pages, and each of them gets frustrated in different ways. Your job this quarter is to make the site work for all of them , page by page, evidence in hand — not in the abstract and not by personal taste. pages.title: Choose Your Track · COGS 160
pages.excerpt: Choose Your Track · COGS 160 K-ATLAS | Schedule Setup Topics Evidence Ask COGS 160 · Spring 2026 COGS 160 · Spring 2026 Choose Your Track Four tracks, four capability gaps, one shared system. Each track produces real components that integrate into Knowledge Atlas. Browse the details below, then sign up. How Tracks Work Weeks 4–7 are track-specific sprints. Each track has its own syllabus, deliverables, and support. You work in teams of three to four students. Each sprint produces a real, tested component that connects to the Knowledge Atlas infrastructure. Your methodology log documents the process: the decisions you made, the obstacles you overcame, and the evidence that justified your choices. Choosing a track is a serious commitment, but the choice reflects your interests and strengths. You have had two weeks to explore. If you have not decided by Week 3, this page and the track assignment pages will help you choose. The track signup process is straightforward: select a track, identify your teammates or indicate that you are willing to be grouped, and wait for formal team assignment. You can change tracks until the end of Week 3. Your job is to translate experimental protocols from the research fronts into navigable, instrumented virtual scenes that could actually be used to run the missing studies. By Week 7 you will produce a fully buildable A-Frame scene with at least three instrumented dependent variables, a design specification document, and a validation report from two naïve-user walkthroughs. Overview Week 3.5 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Setup guide Article intake Shared resources Task manifests KA Contextual Docs Why this week matters Teams often lose two weeks by talking as if the system already exists. In Track 2 that failure is especially easy, because AF has a lot of real components and a lot of surrounding prose. Week 3.5 is therefore a discipline week: identify the real commands, the real boundary, the real tests, and the real missing pieces. Use the repo as ground truth The current AF repo exposes real entry points such as python cli/main.py doctor , stats , classify , discover , and build-jobs . Work from those. Do not inherit imaginary helper scripts from older site text without verifying that they exist. What you need to understand before building AF is not AE AF owns discovery, import, deduplication, classification, intake, and bundle construction. AE owns downstream extraction. pages.excerpt: Track 2 · Week 4 · Article Finder · COGS 160 Track 2 · Week 4 Run one real topic through a first working AF path This week shifts from setup to operation. The point is to prove that one real topic or question can pass through discovery, triage, and first-pass review without dissolving into hand-waving. This week's question Can we run one concrete topic through Article Finder and show which candidates are accepted, which are borderline, which are duplicates, and why? Track 2 Week sequence Work this page before you broaden the system. Overview Week 3.5 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Setup guide Article intake Shared resources Task manifests KA Contextual Docs Why this week matters By the end of this week you should have one genuinely operational example, not a speculative architecture. That example may still be small. Small is acceptable. Unproved is not. Choose one topic and stay with it long enough to learn from it Changing topics every day hides failure. Pick one topic, one question bundle, or one research front and keep enough pressure on it to expose duplicates, weak metadata, and borderline cases. What you need to understand before building Corpus growth and triage quality are different Adding papers is easy. Showing that the added papers are relevant, non-duplicate, and defensibly prioritized is the real work. Borderline papers are informative If a paper sits between reject and send-to-eater, that is where the question filter, constitutions, and review logic become valuable

pages.excerpt: Track 2 · Week 5 · Article Finder · COGS 160 Track 2 · Week 5 Reduce false positives and prove the handoff Now that you have one working path, the next task is to make it less naive: improve deduplication and metadata quality, test the question filter where it matters, and show that the AF → AE handoff artifact is valid. This week's question Can we show that AF does not merely find papers, but filters them intelligently and hands AE a structurally trustworthy bundle? Track 2 Week sequence This week is about quality, not just quantity. Overview Week 3.5 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Article intake Shared resources Task manifests KA Contextual Docs Why this week matters A weak literature system can always make its numbers look better by being permissive. Week 5 is where you stop rewarding permissiveness and start rewarding discrimination: fewer duplicates, cleaner metadata, clearer reasons, and a bundle that AE can process without guessing. What you need to understand before building Deduplication is multi-signal DOI matching is strongest, but title and author overlap matter too. A system that only catches exact DOI matches will miss real duplicates. Metadata quality affects every later stage Bad title, DOI, year, or abstract fields make relevance, bundling, and later extraction less reliable. The handoff schema is not decoration paper.json is the formal contract between AF and AE. If required fields are missing or misleading, the handoff is bad. Borderline cases teach you about

pages.excerpt: Track 2 · Week 6 · Article Finder · COGS 160 Track 2 · Week 6 Make the pipeline robust across topics Week 6 is where you find out whether your improvements generalize. The question is no longer "does it work for one convenient topic?" but "does it stay sensible when we vary the topic, the research front, and the failure modes?" This week's question When we run AF on multiple topics or research fronts, does it remain disciplined about relevance, duplication, logging, and uncertainty? Track 2 Week sequence This week is about robustness, logging, and comparison. Overview Week 3.5 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Setup guide Shared resources Task manifests KA Contextual Docs Why this week matters A pipeline that works on one hand-picked topic may simply be overfit. Week 6 tests whether your logic survives topic changes, adjacent literatures, messy metadata, and the real ambiguity that appears when a corpus grows. Near miss is not the same as topic expansion If your filter surfaces a paper that almost fits the current topic, that is one kind of signal. If it surfaces a paper that suggests the topic boundary itself should expand, that is another. Keep those signals separate. What you need to understand before building Generalization requires comparison You need at least two topics, bundles, or research fronts if you want to say the pipeline behaves consistently. Logging is part of correctness If a rerun duplicates papers or silently changes decisions and you cannot tell why, the system

pages.excerpt: Track 2 · Week 7 · Article Finder · COGS 160 Track 2 · Week 7 Freeze a reproducible demo and say what the system cannot yet do The final week is not about pretending the system is finished. It is about turning the strongest current path into a reproducible demonstration, documenting limits clearly, and leaving the next team with something they can trust. This week's question What can we demonstrate reliably now, under controlled conditions, and what should we explicitly refuse to claim? Track 2 Week sequence End with a reproducible path, not a vague impression. Overview Week 3.5 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Setup guide Article intake KA Contextual Docs Why this week matters A final demo that hides uncertainty damages the project. A final demo that is smaller but reproducible, honest, and well documented moves the project forward. This week is about confidence through constraint. Do not oversell If the pipeline only works reliably on a few topics, say that. If the question filter only has one drafted constitution, say that. The class should be able to trust your limits statement. What you need to understand before building Reproducibility beats surface polish A plain demonstration that another student can rerun is worth more than a polished story no one can verify. Claims require evidence pointers Every claim in the final presentation should point to a command, report, screenshot, benchmark, or bundle. Use AI well Before you ask AI for help, make sure you can state what you are trying to learn this week and what evidence would count as a useful answer. Class meetings Meeting A — Start from the problem, not the tools Start here: Write, in plain language, what the studio is trying to build and what it is refusing to build. In class Individually write a three-sentence answer to the studio problem. In pairs, compare answers and produce a shared version that distinguishes generation from transformation. As a group, make a board with three columns: what must stay stable after an edit, what can change, and what must be measured or logged. Ask an AI assistant to define three unfamiliar terms you encounter during discussion. Then rewrite the definitions in your own words with direct relevance to this studio. End by producing a studio problem statement that everyone can support for the next seven weeks. In class Take two rows from the architectural factor table and break them into manipulable parameters, not just verbal paraphrases. Ask AI for help distinguishing closely related ideas such as perceptual fluency, soft fascination, or refuge; then decide what would count as evidence for each in a room transformation workflow. Draft a template for a factor sheet: name, target latent variables, parameters, allowed range, side effects, validation checks, and example transformations. Exchange sheets with another team and see whether they are implementable or still too abstract. Home Complete factor sheets for the reduced core set. In class Focus on light properties, surface materials and texture, and prospect/refuge. Break the work into three questions: what parameters are controllable, what side effects occur, and how will you show what changed to a non-expert user? Use AI to gather examples of warm versus cool lighting, material substitutions, or refuge cues, but tie every suggestion back to an actual scene edit. In class Collect difficult instructions such as 'increase mystery while preserving navigability' or 'increase awe without making the room cold'. Sort each instruction into what is directly actionable, what is ambiguous, and what requires a follow-up question or a default assumption. Ask AI for alternative phrasings and hidden assumptions. Keep only the parts that improve your factor mapping or error handling. Design a simple translation layer: accepted terms, synonyms, warnings, and structured output. headings: How a journey fills it in
pages.excerpt: Five Answer Shapes — Track 4 Schemata Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 4 / Task 2 / Answer-Shape Catalogue Five Answer Shapes Every question in the corpus is fitted to one of five canonical answer shapes before it becomes a journey. The shapes are not arbitrary decorations — each one is a different commitment about what would satisfy a reader. A theory-prober wants a Toulmin; a newcomer wants a field map; a sensors-seeker wants a procedure; a measures-shopper wants a contrast pair; a busy PI wants a ranked brief. This page visualises each shape with a worked example from the researcher corpus. 1. Toulmin diagram 2. Field map 3. Procedure 4. Contrast pair 5. Ranked brief 1. Toulmin diagram A horizontal inference from data to claim , tagged with a qualifier on the inference arrow itself (e.g., "probably", "usually", "in adult office workers"). The warrant hangs vertically from the inference arrow and states the rule that licenses the move from data to claim. The backing hangs below the warrant and justifies the warrant itself — typically a theoretical framework, a body of prior literature, or a methodological norm. The rebuttal hangs vertically from the claim and names the conditions under which the claim would fail. The geometry is not decoration: reading top-to-bottom on the left tells you why the inference is licensed; reading top-to-bottom on the right tells you what could undercut it. (A

pages.title: Chinn & Brewer (1993) — Seven Responses to Anomalous Data
pages.excerpt: Chinn & Brewer (1993) — Seven Responses to Anomalous Data Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 4 / Task 3 / Chinn & Brewer 7-Response Widget Seven Responses to Anomalous Data Chinn & Brewer (1993) catalogued how readers actually respond when presented with evidence that conflicts with a theory they hold. Their seven-response menu is descriptive, not prescriptive: it tells journey designers what readers do , not what they should do. Use it to anticipate how a reader will receive the evidence on a journey page, and to surface the response options explicitly rather than letting them happen invisibly. Anomalous datum (worked example) A pre-registered replication finds no effect of biophilic indoor plants on perceived restorativeness in office workers, despite a large meta-analytic effect in earlier studies. Worked example: Biophilia replication failure Ceiling-height construal failure Attention restoration vs. affect confound Open all Close all theory-protective · theory-flexible · theory-revising Source: Chinn, C. A., & Brewer, W. F. (1993). The role of anomalous data in knowledge acquisition: A theoretical framework and implications for science instruction. Review of Educational Research , 63(1), 1–49. https://doi.org/10.3102/00346543063001001 Knowledge Atlas, COGS 160 Track 4, v1. pages.title: Does ART survive the affective confound? — A worked evidential journey
headings: Does Attention Restoration Theory survive the affective confound?
Re-analyses controlling for affect attenuate the restoration effect by roughly 40 to 60 per cent.
If two candidate mechanisms are always present together, no analysis that fails to separate them can determine which is doing the work.
Attention Restoration Theory partly survives the affective-confound critique; the mechanism it posits remains underdetermined.
The principal threat to the claim: affect may be a mediator rather than a confounder.
Step 5 · How readers respond to a defeater
The seven responses to anomalous data, applied to the affect-confound critique.
Which response do you take with this defeater, and why?
pages.excerpt: Does ART survive the affective confound? — A worked evidential journey Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 4 / Task 3 / Worked example: Q-29 Knowledge Atlas · Answer-shape catalogue · Worked journey: Q-29 (researcher persona) Does Attention Restoration Theory survive the affective confound? A worked evidential journey · Toulmin shape · researcher persona · Q-29 in the canonical question corpus. ⌕ The reader has typed the question above. The Atlas will now build a Toulmin argument around it, surface the principal defeater inline, and ask the reader which Chinn & Brewer (1993) response they would take with the rebuttal before they leave. Step 1 · Data Re-analyses controlling for affect attenuate the restoration effect by roughly 40 to 60 per cent. The empirical core of the affect-confound critique is a set of re-analyses of attention-restoration studies in which the analyst statistically partials out the post-exposure improvement in mood. The restoration effect — operationalised either as improved performance on attention-demanding tasks or as self-reported restorativeness — does not vanish under this treatment, but it shrinks substantially. Pooled across the studies that report enough detail to be re-analysed, the shrinkage falls in the 40 to 60 per cent range. ~40–60% attenuation of the restoration effect when affect is statistically controlled. pages.excerpt: Track 4 · Week 4 · Interaction Design · COGS 160 Track 4 · Week 4 Journey mapping & persona calibration One page walkthrough is a data point. This week you expand to a triad — three pages chosen to calibrate your panel — and produce the journey maps that will guide the rest of the quarter. This week's question Can your persona panel distinguish between pages that work for your user and pages that fail them — and can you explain why? Track 4 Week sequence Work this page before you broaden. Overview Week 3.5 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Setup guide Full UX overview Persona panel exercise Why this week matters A panel that praises everything or condemns everything is poorly constructed. The calibration triad forces your panel to make discriminations: one page you expect to work, one you expect to fail, one you are genuinely uncertain about. After running all three, you know whether your panel is generating real signal or just narrative. The calibration triad Pick three pages from your assigned cluster: (1) one you predict will work well for your persona, (2) one you predict will frustrate them, (3) one where you are genuinely unsure. Run your panel on all three. If the panel agrees with all your predictions, be suspicious — it may be echoing your expectations rather than testing them. headings: How to use AI this week
pages.excerpt: Track 4 · Week 5 · Interaction Design · COGS 160 Track 4 · Week 5 Systematic heuristic audits Journey maps showed you where your persona gets stuck. This week you diagnose why — systematically, using the 35-dimension heuristic framework, across your entire page cluster. This week's question Which heuristic violations are most severe, most frequent, and most fixable in your page cluster — and what is the evidence for each? Track 4 Week sequence Work this page before you broaden. Overview Week 3.5 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Setup guide Full UX overview Persona panel exercise Why this week matters Journey maps are narrative. Heuristic audits are systematic. You need both. The audit forces you to evaluate every page in your cluster against the same 35 criteria, which prevents you from fixating on the first problem you noticed and missing the pattern that connects twelve smaller problems across pages. Prioritise by severity × frequency A major violation that appears on 12 pages matters more than a catastrophic violation on one obscure page. The audit report must rank findings, not just list them. The 35-dimension framework The K-ATLAS usability critic uses three established frameworks. You do not need to memorise all 35 dimensions, but you must apply them systematically to each page. Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (H1–H10) ID Heuristic One-line test H1 Visibility of system status Does the user always know where they are and what is happening? pages.excerpt: Track 4 · Week 6 · Interaction Design · COGS 160 Track 4 · Week 6 Fix sprints & cross-persona review Diagnosis is done. This week you transition from finding problems to solving them — and proving that your solutions actually work for your persona without breaking the experience for someone else's. This week's question Can you implement your top-severity fixes in clean Pull Requests, and can another student's persona still use the page after your changes? Track 4 Week sequence Work this page before you broaden. Overview Week 3.5 Week 4 Week 5 Week 6 Week 7 Setup guide Full UX overview Persona panel exercise Why this week matters Finding problems is necessary but not sufficient. This week you demonstrate that you can fix real usability issues in production code, submit them as reviewable Pull Requests, and verify that fixes for one persona do not create new problems for another. Cross-persona review is mandatory Every PR gets reviewed by a student holding a different persona. A navigation fix that helps the Practitioner (who wants to get in and out fast) might remove contextual information that the Researcher needs. The cross-review catches these trade-offs before they ship. Types of fixes Navigation & information architecture Missing breadcrumbs, inconsistent back-links, dead-end pages, unclear active-state indicators, broken or misleading labels. These are often CSS + HTML fixes with high impact. Interactivity Broken or missing search/filter, non-functional buttons, localSto

pages.title: Week 2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough · COGS 160
headings: Before You Begin: Three Things You Need
pages.excerpt: Week 2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough · COGS 160 K-ATLAS | Schedule Exercises Walkthrough Evidence COGS 160 · Spring 2026 COGS 160 · Week 2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough Follow these steps exactly. No programming experience required. The only part that requires your own thinking is composing the prompts you send to the AI. What This Assignment Actually Is You are going to build a working software component — a real piece of the Knowledge Atlas system — without writing a single line of code yourself. An AI will write all the code. Your job is something harder and more important: you will direct the AI by thinking carefully about what the component should do before asking the AI to build it. This matters because AI writes code fast but thinks about requirements poorly. If you say "build me a search filter," it will — and the result will look professional, seem to work, and quietly fail on half the cases that matter. The component will search the wrong fields, display misleading numbers, or crash on edge cases that any thoughtful person would have anticipated. The AI's code is only as good as the instructions you give it. The assignment teaches you a method for giving those instructions. It has seven steps. Steps 1 through 3 happen before any code is written — they are about thinking, not programming. Steps 4 through 7 are about building and testing. The entire process takes 60–80 minutes. You will submit a document showing every step, including your mistakes. Here is the sequence: The sev

Tuesday: Cross-track Testing Thursday: Sprint 1 Demos Methodology Log #3 Tuesday · Day 9 · Cross-track Testing Cross-Track Testing You have spent the last week building. Now you encounter a fundamental principle of verification: testing your own work finds some bugs, but having someone else test your work finds the bugs your familiarity blinds you to. This is why Step 6 of the workflow exists as a separate step — and why someone other than the implementer should perform it. Today you will swap your deliverable with another team and test their work against their acceptance criteria. You will discover failures that the original team could not see. 1 Swap Components with Another Team You will be paired with another team — either on the same track or a different track. Each team passes their Sprint 1 deliverable to the other team. You receive the acceptance criteria from the other team so you know exactly what you should be testing. Take their deliverable and run it through their contract. For each acceptance criterion, test whether their implementation passes or fails. 2 Write Up Test Results Document what passes and what fails. Tuesday: Cross-track Integration Thursday: Sprint 2 Demos Methodology Log #4 Tuesday · Day 13 · Integration Testing Integration Testing Across Tracks Knowledge Atlas is not four independent tracks. It is a unified system where each track's output feeds into another track's input. The Image Tagger produces tagged images. The Article Finder produces vetted articles. The VR Production track converts articles into spatial experiences. The GUI Evaluation track measures how well the interface serves users. Today you discover whether these pieces actually fit together. 1 Cross-Track Integration Test Each track's Sprint 2 output feeds into at least one other track. You will perform the integration test: does Track 2's article data feed correctly into Track 1's tagging interface? Does Track 4's GUI correctly display Track 1's tagged images? Does the data format produced by one track match the data format expected by the next track? If not, who needs to change their code — the producer or the consumer? This is where you discover contract misalignment. 2 Fix Integration Failures The most common problem at integration boundaries is vocabulary mismatch. pages.title: Article · Knowledge Atlas
headings: The Benefits of Daylight through Windows
Theories cited and used in the article
§8 How valuable is it to work on this topic?
pages.excerpt: Article · Knowledge Atlas The Benefits of Daylight through Windows Boyce, P., Hunter, C., & Howlett, O. Lighting Research Center, Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute · 2003 · Capturing the Daylight Dividend Program · paper_id: PDF-0011 6.4 EN score ★ 71 st percentile Why this number? Comprehensive literature review (not a primary study) covering daylight, attention, mood, alertness, and circadian effects. Strong on synthesis and breadth; loses points for being a narrative review without meta-analytic aggregation, and for resting on a small primary sample ( n = 22) for some quantitative claims. Effect-size estimate for the headline daylight-on-performance link is small ( d ≈ 0.16) but consistently positive across the cited studies. See the full warrant breakdown → §1 Science summary The paper begins from a tension that defines the daylighting literature: treating daylight as a substitute for electric lighting cannot, on its own, justify the architectural cost of large or carefully-placed windows. The authors therefore reframe the question. The relevant payoff is not energy saved, it is human performance gained — attention, alertness, mood, satisfaction, and the long-arc circadian outcomes that show up as sleep, health, and absenteeism in the buildings whose occupants spend their days inside them. With that framing in place, Boyce, Hunter, and Howlett conduct a structured review of the empirical evidence for four channels through which daylight can plausibly affect occupants. The

pages.excerpt: Suggest a paper — Knowledge Atlas Suggest a paper Help us index a study we missed Knowledge Atlas catalogues ~ 1,400 peer-reviewed studies on architecture, environment, and cognition. If you know of a study we should add, tell us about it — usually takes under a minute. Why it's useful Every paper we index strengthens the Atlas in three ways: Better coverage : a topic bundle with six papers is more defensible than one with three. Better contradictions : papers that disagree with existing evidence sharpen the picture. Better synthesis : more primary-source breadth lets the system generate more honest mechanism and theory summaries. You don't need to tag anything, classify anything, or format the metadata. Drop the PDF or the citation and we'll take it from there. Submit The fastest path: upload a PDF. If you don't have a copy at hand, a DOI or a pasted citation also works. Or paste a citation, DOI, or title APA is nice but not required — a DOI or a title + year is enough. Why this paper matters (optional) Your email (optional) We will only use this to reply about this submission. We don't add you to any list. pages.excerpt: Course Design Archive — COGS 160 Pre-4-Track Era Course Design Archive · Knowledge Atlas COGS 160 — Pre-4-Track Era Design History What we built before the track model, why it was superseded, and what ideas should carry forward Archived: 2026-03-24 · Owner: David Kirsh, UCSD Cognitive Science · Status: Read-only historical record Archive Notice. The pages documented here represent the first-generation COGS 160 site design, built before the 4-track contributor model (Track 1: Image Tagging; Track 2: Article Finding; Track 3: VR Production; Track 4: GUI Evaluation & Experiment Design) was adopted as the course structure in March 2026. The files remain accessible in Knowledge_Atlas/Designing_Experiments/ and are linked from the current KA pages via "Previous pages" footer links. They must not be deleted — they contain research data and design ideas of continuing value. Why the Redesign Happened The original COGS 160 site was organised around research topics (the 20 CNFA clusters) and disciplinary categories (acoustic, thermal, social, biophilic, cognitive). Students navigated a landscape of findings and were expected to self-direct into experiment design. In practice this was too open-ended: students needed more structured role assignments, clearer deliverables, and a clearer sense of what they were contributing to a collective project. The 4-track model solves this by assigning each student to a concrete production role — tagger, article finder, VR producer, or GUI evaluator —

pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — Article Finder Track – Planning & Deliverables Article Finder Track – AF-Track Planning & Deliverables Smart RAG Integration, VOI-to-Query Pipeline, and Research Contribution Infrastructure ← All Tracks ⚙️ Setup Guide Week 3 (D1) Week 4 (D2) Week 5 (D2) Week 6 (D3) Week 7 (D4) Timeline Section 1: Track Overview What Is the AF Track? The Article Finder (AF) Track is a 6-week research engineering project that tracks down and retrieves relevant scientific articles to expand the ATLAS research corpus. Your team (6 students, 3 pairs) will build VOI-driven search queries, retrieve articles via RAG platforms and open databases, and create the infrastructure for researchers to contribute their own PDFs. At the end of the 6 weeks, you'll have built a functional component of the ATLAS/Knowledge Accelerator system that enables researchers to discover and contribute evidence-backed findings. How AF Contributes to ATLAS/KA The broader ATLAS project aims to build a coherence-based knowledge system for computational epistemology. The Article Finder track addresses a critical bottleneck: how do we systematically discover evidence to fill gaps in the system's knowledge? Your work produces: Smart RAG Evaluation Report: Comparison of Consensus, Elicit, Scite, Semantic Scholar, and OpenAlex for evidence discovery VOI-to-Query Bridge: A translation layer that converts research questions into optimized search queries for multiple backends Public Contribution System: Interface fo

pages.title: COGS160: AF Track — Week 3 · D1: Pipeline Orientation
headings: You Will Know How to Do These Five Things
The AF Module: Function, Structure, and Key Contracts

D1 Task Sequence — What to Do This Week
pages.excerpt: COGS160: AF Track — Week 3 · D1: Pipeline Orientation Week 3 — D1: Pipeline Orientation Article Finder Track · COGS 160: AI & VR D1 · 5% of final grade · Due end of Week 3 ← AF Track Overview Setup Guide Week 4 → ATLAS is a question-answering system for neuroarchitecture — it answers questions like "Does biophilic design reduce cortisol levels?" by synthesizing evidence from dozens of scientific papers. But ATLAS can only answer questions about evidence it has already extracted. When a researcher asks about a topic that the corpus doesn't cover, ATLAS doesn't say "I don't know" — it gives a low-confidence answer based on whatever loosely related beliefs it holds. That answer can mislead. The Article Finder track exists because the solution to that problem is not better AI reasoning — it is more evidence. Your team's job is to build the pipeline that finds new articles, evaluates their relevance, and routes them into the ATLAS extraction workflow. Before you can build it, you need to understand exactly what the pipeline already does, where its knowledge gaps are, and what it expects from incoming articles. D1 is that orientation. By the end of this week, you will have read the six key source files that define the AF pipeline, traced one query end-to-end through the system, and written a report that your team will use as a shared reference for the next seven weeks. You Will Know How to Do These Five Things Navigate Trace any natural-language query through QueryParser → QueryEng

pages.title: Week 4: Platform Evaluation – Article Finder Track | COGS160
headings: The Complication: Same Question, Different Answers
The Epistemic Question That Makes This Week Important
pages.excerpt: Week 4: Platform Evaluation – Article Finder Track | COGS160 Week 4: Five Platforms, One Question Which RAG System Actually Finds the Evidence? ← AF Track Overview All Tracks Tasks FAQ The Complication: Same Question, Different Answers You search five different platforms for evidence about whether biophilic design reduces stress. Consensus returns 87 relevant papers with a 72% consensus finding. Scite returns 34 papers, but with more nuanced citation context: some papers support the claim, others challenge it. Semantic Scholar returns 143 papers sorted by citation count, not relevance. OpenAlex returns everything published with those keywords, regardless of rigor. The evidence did not change between platforms. The platforms changed what counts as evidence. This week you discover exactly where those differences emerge—not in the databases themselves, but in how they index, rank, and present research. By the end of this week, you will have tested all five platforms systematically and produced a comparative matrix that makes the invisible differences visible. Your Usability Evaluation Report (D2, Part 1) documents this discovery. It is not a consumer review. It is a technical investigation of how platform design choices—indexing strategy, ranking algorithm, interface simplicity, result filtering—shape which research questions get answered. What You Will Know By Friday Which platform returns results fastest. Which one shows abstracts (and which makes you click to see them). Which

The Deeper Question: Platform Selection as Epistemology
pages.excerpt: Week 5: Recall/Precision & D2 Completion – Article Finder Track | COGS160 Week 5: The Platform You Trust Shapes What ATLAS Believes Citation Intersection, Recall Analysis & Consensus Validation ← AF Track Overview ← Week 4 All Tracks Tasks D2 Full Report The Insight: Platform Choice Is Epistemological Choice Last week you tested interfaces. This week you test what the platforms actually find. A claim can have strong evidence in Scite (many papers support it, few dispute it) while appearing weaker in Elicit (fewer high-quality studies meet the inclusion criteria) simultaneously. Neither platform is wrong. They index and rank differently. When ATLAS chooses to integrate Semantic Scholar instead of Consensus, ATLAS is not choosing a tool. It is choosing which evidence gets counted as evidence. This week's work makes that choice deliberate and defensible. You will measure each platform's recall (did it find the papers we know exist?) and precision (when it returned results, how many were actually relevant?). You will test the Consensus confidence meter: when it says 70% of studies support a claim, does the citation distribution actually show 70% support? You will identify papers that one platform finds and another misses entirely, and ask: what indexing decision explains that gap? By Friday, you will have the data needed to write D2, the full Usability Evaluation Report (8–10 pages). This report will sit at the center of ATLAS's platform selection decision. It is not just a class

pages.excerpt: Week 6: Recall/Precision Analysis – Article Finder Track | COGS160 Week 6: A Platform That Misses Half the Evidence Isn't Neutral — It's Wrong ← AF Track Overview All Tracks Tasks Grading FAQ The Complication: Invisible Gaps in Evidence You search for papers on "attention restoration" and find 47 results. You think you have the evidence. But an expert researcher, conducting the same search manually on Google Scholar and citation networks, finds 73 papers—26 of which the platform missed entirely. Those 26 papers include critical boundary conditions (restoration works better in certain landscapes), mechanism studies (why attention recovers), and contradictions (environments that fail to restore). ATLAS doesn't know those papers exist. When ATLAS estimates the strength of evidence for "exposure to natural light improves office worker attention," it calculates from the 47 papers you found. If the 26 missing papers show weaker or contradictory results, ATLAS arrives at an inflated confidence estimate. The platform did not change the evidence. The platform determined what counts as evidence. This week moves you from qualitative observation (Week 5: "Scite is harder to use than Consensus") to quantitative measurement. You will build a gold-standard reference set of papers that a domain expert agrees are relevant to three core questions. Then you will measure how many of those gold papers each platform actually finds. This number—recall—is the answer to a simple question: Does this p

pages.excerpt: Week 7: Improvement Proposal – Article Finder Track | COGS160 Week 7: The Gap Is Real. Now Design a Bridge. From Evaluation to Architecture ← AF Track Overview All Tracks Tasks Grading FAQ The Complication: No Single Platform Covers the Literature Your D3 measurements showed it clearly: Semantic Scholar finds 79% of papers on "natural light → attention" but only 43% on "computational modeling of restoration." Consensus returns high-precision results but misses older foundational work. Scite shows citations in context but doesn't index design literature. No platform excels at everything. The question is no longer "Which platform should ATLAS use?" The answer is "All of them, strategically." This week moves from measurement to design. You will architect a VOI-to-Query Bridge: a system that takes a belief gap from ATLAS ("insufficient evidence for attention recovery from natural light in older adults") and translates it into optimized, platform-specific queries, executes them across multiple platforms intelligently, and returns a deduplicated, ranked candidate list that ATLAS integrates into its evidence corpus. This bridge is not a finished product. It is a technical specification—a blueprint for Week 8's implementation. A good spec makes implementation straightforward. A vague spec means Week 8 bogs down in ambiguity. Your job: make your choice concrete and defensible. What You Will Deliver A 10–12 page technical proposal that a developer (Week 8 student or external engineer)

pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track: Expert Design Panel Reference ← GUI Track Overview | Week 5: Expert Panel | Visualization Guide → Track 4 · GUI Reference · GUI Track · COGS160 Spring 2026 Expert Design Panel Reference Verbatim advice from the canonical voices in HCI, UX, and interaction design — and how each argument shaped the ATLAS interface you are auditing. Status: Permanent reference — use throughout Weeks 3–10 Relevance: All GUI track deliverables Companion: Visualization Design Guide How to use this document. This page records, verbatim, the arguments that the expert panel made when consulted during the design of the ATLAS user-home and workflow-hub pages (March 2026). Each expert's position is stated in their own terms — not summarised into bullet points — because the reasoning matters as much as the conclusion. When you write your usability critiques and success conditions, you should cite these experts by name with APA references from the list at the bottom of this page. A critique that cites Norman on affordances or Shneiderman on closure is more persuasive than one that says "it was confusing." A companion page, Visualization Design Guide , covers data display, chart choice, and Tufte's data-ink principle for the ATLAS dashboard and evidence cards. Panel Roster The panel spans three generations of HCI and UX scholarship, from the founding cognitive ergonomists of the 1980s through the contemporary practitioners who shaped modern web and mobile design. 🧠 Don Norman C

Track Overview & Learning Objectives Big Picture : The ATLAS system is built for multiple user types—researchers, architects, domain experts, administrators, students, and the general public. But not all of them experience the interface well. Your job is to systematically evaluate ATLAS for usability across all these user types, identify the pain points, propose improvements, and prototype AI-powered help systems that make the interface more effective. By the end of this track, you'll deliver a comprehensive usability evaluation and improvement roadmap that will guide development for the Fall 2026 COGS160: AI & VR course. pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track: Visualization Design Guide ← GUI Track Overview | Expert Panel Reference | Week 7: Final Report → Track 4 · GUI Reference · GUI Track · COGS160 Spring 2026 Visualization Design Guide The canon of data visualization scholarship — from Tufte's data-ink principle to contemporary chart choice frameworks — applied to the ATLAS dashboard, evidence cards, and argument maps you are auditing. Status: Permanent reference — especially relevant for Weeks 5–7 and Phase 3 Companion: Expert Panel Reference Why visualization? ATLAS is a data-heavy system. Its dashboard displays article coverage statistics, evidence quality ratings, argument graphs, and tag distributions. Its evidence cards encode study quality metadata. Its interpretation pages display probability assessments. Every one of these is a data visualization — and every one can be done well or badly. The difference between a well-designed evidence card and a poorly designed one is not aesthetic: it is epistemic. A card that misrepresents effect size confidence, obscures sample heterogeneity, or presents contested findings as settled is not just ugly — it is misleading. The visualization literature has produced a body of principles that are as well-established as anything in HCI. Your audit of the ATLAS data displays should apply these principles systematically. When you find a violation, articulate it in terms of the principle it violates and the scholar who established it. 📐 Edward Tufte — The Visual

pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track Week 3: User Type Profiles ← Course Home 🖥 GUI Evaluation Track Track Overview → Week 3 · Deliverable D1 · Pair 1 leads Who Is This System Actually For? Building Refined User Type Profiles and a Use-Case Matrix Due: Sunday, Week 3 (11:59 PM) Weight: 12% of course grade Lead: Pair 1 (all pairs contribute recordings) Here is a fact about usability testing that most people find surprising: the hardest part is not running the tests. It is deciding whose experience you are trying to improve. The Article Eater system has at least eight distinct user types — from first-year undergraduates navigating a course assignment to senior researchers querying a knowledge base developed over years. Each user brings a different mental model, a different vocabulary for describing what the system does, and a different definition of "it worked." A usability test that treats these eight users as interchangeable will produce findings that are simultaneously true of no one in particular and therefore actionable for no one at all. This week, Pair 1 constructs the foundation that all subsequent GUI work depends on: a detailed map of who uses the system, what they are trying to accomplish, and where they get stuck. Everyone in the track contributes at least one recorded think-aloud session, but Pair 1 analyzes the recordings and produces the final profiles. Learning Outcomes 1 Distinguish eight user types by their goals, prior knowledge, and relationship to the system — and

pages.title: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track Week 4: AI Capability Boundary
headings: Part 1 — What "AI Help" Actually Means (And Why It Fails Differently)
pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track Week 4: AI Capability Boundary ← Course Home 🖥 GUI Evaluation Track Track Overview → Week 4 · Deliverable D2 · Pair 2 leads Where AI Help Breaks Mapping AI Capability Boundaries and Designing Context-Sensitive Help Due: Sunday, Week 4 (11:59 PM) Weight: 12% of course grade Lead: Pair 2 (all pairs contribute probe tasks) A human expert, asked a question outside their domain, will say "I don't know." They establish a boundary. An AI language model, asked the same question, will produce a confident-sounding answer that may be catastrophically wrong — and the answer will be wrong in a way that is difficult for a non-expert to detect . This asymmetry is not a small usability problem. It is the central design problem for any interface that integrates AI assistance. Evidence from recent industry studies indicates that users equipped with AI help are often less accurate than users without it, because the AI's false confidence overwrites the user's own caution (Weld & Bansal, 2019). This week, Pair 2 turns this failure mode into a design opportunity. You will systematically probe the Article Eater's help capacity — what it handles well, what it fails at, and how those failures look from a user perspective — then design a help architecture that compensates for the boundary. The key methodological innovation is the "first-timer rule": at least one person testing the AI help must encounter it with no prior knowledge of what it is supposed to do. Learning Outc
