pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track Week 5: Expert Panel Critique ← Course Home 🖥 GUI Evaluation Track Track Overview → Week 5 · Deliverable D3 · Pair 3 leads When Algorithms and Experts Disagree Comparing Automated UI Evaluation to Human Expert Judgment Due: Sunday, Week 5 (11:59 PM) Weight: 15% of course grade Lead: Pair 3 (all pairs contribute evaluation sessions) You might reasonably expect that automated UI evaluation—the application of systematic rules to interface design—would be more objective than human judgment. Rules are applied consistently, without fatigue, without bias, without the variability that plagues human evaluation. In one narrow sense, this expectation is correct. But Ivory and Hearst (2001) document a crucial irony: automated evaluation systems that apply consistent rules to an incomplete rule set produce systematically wrong answers in exactly the situations where you need the most reliable judgment. The automation does not eliminate subjectivity; it disguises subjectivity as objectivity. This week, Pair 3 becomes a critical reader of automated evaluation. You will run the Article Eater's UI evaluation agent on four user-type-specific tasks, then conduct an independent human expert evaluation of the same tasks. You will compare the two systematically, document where and why they diverge, and propose what the automated agent is missing. Your job is not to trust the agent or to dismiss it—it is to understand its actual strengths and limitations. Learning Outco

pages.title: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track Week 6: Personalization & AI Help System
headings: Worked Example: The VOI Query Checkpoint
pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track Week 6: Personalization & AI Help System ← Course Home 🖥 GUI Evaluation Track Track Overview → Week 6 — Deliverable D4 Personalization & AI Help System Proposal Shifting from topic-based to context-sensitive, adaptive help Due: Friday, Week 6 (11:59 PM) Weight: 11% of GUI track Team: Pair 2 Deliverable Type: Proposal + Prototype Intellectual Challenge Most help systems are designed as if all users have identical questions at identical moments. Real users don't. An enrolled biology student asking "How do I compare species using this tool?" needs different help than a guest scientist asking "What datasets are available?" at the same interface location. The intellectual core of this deliverable is recognizing that help must be context-sensitive — determined not just by what button the user pressed, but by who they are, what task they are performing, and where they stand in their task progression. The Design Failure Behind the Question Mark. The help button on most software is a last resort — a button users press after they have already given up trying to figure something out themselves. This is a design failure, not a user failure. Help that arrives after the user has given up serves only as confirmation that the task was theoretically possible. Help that arrives at the moment of decision — before the user commits to the wrong action, at the precise checkpoint where confusion is most likely — changes outcomes. Your task is to design a help system tha

pages.title: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track Week 7: Full Usability Evaluation Report
pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — GUI Track Week 7: Full Usability Evaluation Report ← Course Home 🖥 GUI Evaluation Track Track Overview → Week 7 — Deliverable D5 Full Usability Evaluation Report Synthesis of five weeks of user research into a coherent argument Due: Friday, Week 7 (11:59 PM) Weight: 20% of GUI track Team: All Pairs (Coordinated) Deliverable Type: Research Report Intellectual Challenge Three pairs have worked in parallel over five weeks, each seeing different slices of the system. Pair 1 documented user types and their moments of confusion. Pair 2 evaluated what AI could add. Pair 3 modeled expert judgment. You—all three pairs together—must now construct a unified argument from these fragments. A report that lists findings is a catalog. A report that explains what the findings mean, why they occurred, and what they predict about user behavior in future enrollment cycles is an argument. This deliverable demands the latter. From Data to Understanding. Usability testing produces observations: "Six students entered invalid VOI queries." Raw observations are useless without interpretation. Why did they fail? Because they misunderstood VOI as a button to press rather than a comparison framework? Because the interface labels were ambiguous? Because they were rushing? Because they had never seen a Bayesian utility function before? The same observation (six failed queries) generates different redesigns depending on which explanation is true. pages.excerpt: Track 1: Image Tagger | COGS160: AI & VR Track 1: Image Tagger & Scientific Tag Validation Building the CNFA Environmental Psychology Image Tagging System COGS160: AI & VR Spring 2026 | Team Size: 3 Pairs (6 Students) ← Back to All Tracks 1. Every Design Decision About the Built Environment Is Also a Claim About Perception When an architect chooses to cover a wall in rough stone rather than smooth plaster, they are betting — usually implicitly — that the texture will register differently in the nervous systems of the people who inhabit the space. Sometimes that bet is supported by environmental psychology research. More often it is not, because the research exists but the connection between the lab finding and the design decision has never been made explicit. ATLAS exists to make those connections explicit, using a corpus of environmental images tagged with the psychological constructs that decades of research have shown to matter: biophilic richness, spatial openness, luminance uniformity, fractal complexity, and 420 others. Your track builds the tagging layer. Specifically, you implement the 424 scientific tags as computational extractors — algorithms that take a photograph and return a number between 0 and 1 — and validate that those numbers correlate with what the tags actually claim to measure. This is harder than it sounds, and that difficulty is the intellectual content of the track. The registry lists 30 tags as phase_0 (extractable from a 2D photograph without depth

pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — Image Track Week 3: Registry Augmentation ← Course Home 🖼 Image Tagger Track Track Overview ⚙️ Setup Guide 🏗 Tagger Architecture 📋 Tag Browser (424 tags) ← D1a: Tag Triage Week 3 · Assignment D1b The Registry Speaks One Language. You Will Add a Second. Every scientific tag in the CNFA registry has a precise technical definition. None of them carry the plain-English words a participant uses when describing what they see — the words a vision model was trained on. This assignment builds that bridge. Due: Sunday, Week 3 (11:59 PM) Weight: 5% of course grade Team size: 2 students Deliverable: Augmented registry entries (10 tags) as JSON + one visual mapping diagram Ask a study participant to look at a photograph and describe it, and they will say something like "it feels cramped in there" or "there's a nice calm quality to it" or "it's a bit busy." They will not say "this image has a luminance variance of 0.62 and a fractal dimension of 1.34." The registry was built by researchers who speak the second language. Participants — and the vision models trained on human-generated descriptions — speak the first. For the tagging system to produce values that predict how people actually experience environments, both languages must live in the same registry entry. There is also a validation reason. The pipeline checks each scientific extractor against VLM common-sense ratings: does a high fractal_dimension score correlate with images that VLMs call "complex" or "intricate

Due: Sunday, Week 3 (11:59 PM) Weight: 8% of course grade Team size: 2 students Deliverable: Triage table + one Tag Passport draft The Image Tagger track eventually produces a computational tag library for the CNFA corpus — 424 scientific tags, each returning a continuous value between 0 and 1 for any photograph in the database. Your team's job this week is not to implement any of those tags. It is to understand 20 of them well enough to decide which ones a photograph can actually answer — and to understand why that question is harder than it first appears. Here is what makes it hard. A tag like spatial_openness sounds straightforward until you ask: open in what sense? Open the way a meadow is open — which you perceive through peripheral vision, parallax, and the sound of distant wind? Or open the way a building renders in an architectural photograph — which a pixel array can measure? These are different things. Your job is to find out, for each of 20 tags, which kind of question is actually being asked. That investigation — not the coding — is the central skil

pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — Image Track Week 5: Tag Extractors ← All Tracks Track 1 — Image Tagger Week 6: VLM Extractors → Week 5 of 10 | Image Tagger Track Each Extractor Is a Scientific Claim. The Test Suite Is Your Evidence. Implementing a CNFA tag is not a coding exercise. It is an argument: "this computation measures this psychological construct, because here is what it does to images we know the answer for." This week you write both the argument and its proof. Deliverable: D2 — Test Suite + Math Implementations Weight: 15% of final grade Due: End of Week 5 (Sunday 11:59 PM) Team: Pair work — one shared GitHub branch per pair In Week 3 you triaged 20 tags and augmented 10 registry entries with bilingual fields. In Week 4 you established what VLMs can and cannot do reliably. This week you write the deterministic extractors — the math-based ones that can be tested against known ground truth. VLM-based semantic extractors come in Week 6, after you understand what deterministic computation looks like and where it stops working. The central requirement is test-before-code. Write the tests first. This is not a stylistic preference; it is a methodological one. Hagerhall et al. (2004) showed that fractal dimension predicts landscape preference — but only when computed correctly on images with sufficient resolution and appropriate edge detection. If you write the extractor first and then check whether the tests pass, you will unconsciously tune the extractor to pass the tests rather than

pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR Student Tracks | UC San Diego COGS160: AI & VR Student Tracks and Deliverables Spring 2026 | Professor David Kirsh | UC San Diego About the Tracks COGS160: AI & VR is organized around four complementary research tracks that together build and validate the ATLAS system — a web-based platform for organizing and analyzing evidence from environmental psychology. Each track involves 3 pairs of students (6 students per track) working on distinct but interconnected challenges over Weeks 3–7. The tracks are: Image Tagger (scientific tagging of architectural interiors), Article Finder (evaluating the evidence pipeline), VR Stimulus (designing validated experimental stimuli), and GUI Evaluation (improving the interface for all user types). Each track has its own learning objectives, team assignments, and deliverable timeline. Click on any track below to view the full planning document. Repo: Designing_Experiments (github.com/Tag-UCSD/Designing_Experiments) Weight: 20% of final grade Due Thu: Walkthrough log pushed to your branch What Weeks 8–10 are for. Tracks 1–4 built the research infrastructure (image tagger, article finder, VR stimuli, GUI prototype) in Weeks 3–7. Now you switch roles: instead of being a builder, you are a tester and documentarian. Your job is to simulate the experience of a student who will take the Fall 2026 version of this course — a student who has never seen these pages before — and record everything that is broken, confusing, missing, or incorrect. The deliverable is not a critique: it is a working fix. You will leave the site section better than you found it, with every function documented and every tested path either working or explicitly flagged as not yet implemented. Section Assignments The Designing_Experiments site is divided into four sections. Your instructor will assign your team to a section on Tuesday of Week 8. Each section covers a different track's pages — you are auditing pages you did not build, which means you come to them with genuine

These two tasks reinforce each other: writing a success condition often reveals whether a function is actually working correctly. pages.excerpt: COGS160 — Phase 3 Week 10: Final Walkthroughs and Hand-Off ← All Tracks Week 8: Walkthrough ← Week 9: Bug Fixes PHASE 3 · FINAL WEEK Phase 3 · Week 10 · Final Week · All Teams Final Walkthroughs, Hand-Off Report, and Presentations Re-run the simulation on the now-partially-debugged site. Write the hand-off document. Present your section to the class. Open a PR to the upstream repo. Repo: Designing_Experiments Due Tue: Re-walkthrough complete, PR opened Due Thu: Final hand-off report pushed; in-class presentation (5–8 min) The test of whether your work was meaningful is the re-walkthrough. In Week 8 you documented what was broken. In Week 9 you fixed what you could. Now you repeat the simulation from scratch — fresh eyes — and measure the difference. If a naïve Fall student can now navigate your section without the obstacles you documented, your contribution is real. If they still cannot, you know precisely what remains and why. The hand-off document is what the Fall instructor receives. It is the artefact that outlives your participation in this project. Tuesday: Second Student Simulation Walkthrough Repeat the exact same walkthrough protocol you used in Week 8. Start at the track overview page for your section. Attempt every task without using knowledge from Weeks 8–9. Use a fresh browser tab with no localStorage or session state from your development work. pages.title: COGS160: AI & VR — VR Track Weeks 6–7 – Adaptive Preference Testing & Analysis
headings: VR Track – Weeks 6–7: Adaptive Preference Testing & Analysis
Step 5: Write D3 Preference Data Analysis Report
pages.excerpt: COGS160: AI & VR — VR Track Weeks 6–7 – Adaptive Preference Testing & Analysis VR Track – Weeks 6–7: Adaptive Preference Testing & Analysis Validating Your Stimulus Set Through Bayesian Paired Comparisons ← Week 5 All Tracks Week 8 → Context & Scope Input: Three validated scenes from Weeks 4–5 (Scene 1, 3, 5; passed confound audit and technical QA). Output (Week 6): Preference data from ≥8 participants (≥40 paired comparisons per participant = ≥320 total decisions). Output (Week 7): D3 Preference Data Analysis Report (formal deliverable, 20% of final grade). Weeks 6–7 validate that your stimulus dimension actually produces measurable preference differences and that your three stimulus levels are appropriately spaced. You will collect preference judgments from human participants using adaptive Bayesian paired-comparison methodology, then analyze the resulting preference function to assess whether your design successfully operationalizes your construct. Adaptive Preference Testing: Overview What Is Adaptive Preference Testing? Adaptive preference testing uses Bayesian paired comparisons to efficiently estimate participant preferences across your stimulus dimension. Rather than asking every participant to judge all pairs (which is time-consuming), the system adapts: after each decision, it updates a posterior belief about where the participant's preference threshold lies, then selects the next most informative pair to compare. The Key Insight: A participant's preference function

Experiment Builder Wizard COGS 160: AI and VR for Science This wizard helps you design a rigorous experiment in environment–cognition science. It draws on a knowledge base of 5,027 empirical findings from 258 papers to identify where new experiments would be most valuable and to guide you through choosing the right methodology. You'll walk through five steps: (1) choosing a research topic ranked by scientific value, (2) selecting an experimental paradigm suited to your question, (3) analyzing potential confounds, (4) specifying your stimuli for VR implementation, and (5) generating a complete experiment protocol. At every step, click the ℹ️ help buttons to learn more about why each decision matters. The wizard teaches experimental design as you use it. pages.title: Hypothesis Builder — COGS 160
headings: What Level of Hypothesis Are You Aiming For?
pages.excerpt: Hypothesis Builder — COGS 160 COGS 160 Knowledge Navigator Theory & Design Hypothesis Builder Methods Taxonomy Measurement Instruments Sensor Catalogue Epistemic Network Bayesian Network Experiment Builder System Inventory 1. Explore Research Fronts → 2. Learn the Theories → 3. Formulate Hypothesis → 4. Choose Method → 5. Design Protocol Formulate Your Hypothesis A hypothesis is not a guess. It is a specific, falsifiable set of predictions about what you will observe — grounded in what you know about the system you are studying. That grounding can come from deep theory, from measurement science, or from well-established empirical regularities. In the behavioral sciences, all three are legitimate starting points. Before You Begin From Interest to Testable Predictions Students often arrive at this step with a topic of interest and call it a hypothesis. "I'm interested in how nature affects mood" is not a hypothesis — it is the starting point of a journey that ends with one. Understanding the stages between an interest and a set of testable predictions is essential, because each stage represents a different degree of intellectual commitment, and experiments can only test the final form. The journey from interest to testable predictions passes through several stages, each more specific than the last: 1 Interest or Topic An interest is a domain you find compelling. It has no structure, no commitment, no testable content. It is where curiosity begins — and where many students stop.

headings: Measurement Triangulation: Why Three Modalities Are Better Than One
pages.excerpt: Measurement Instruments & Sensors — COGS 160 COGS 160 Knowledge Navigator Theory & Design Hypothesis Builder Methods Taxonomy Measurement Instruments Sensor Catalogue Epistemic Network Bayesian Network Experiment Builder System Inventory Measurement Instruments & Sensors Understand the strengths, weaknesses, and theoretical alignment of each measurement approach. Every instrument is a lens—choose yours carefully. Self-Report Measures Behavioral & Cognitive Tasks Physiological & Neural Measures Choosing Your Measures PANAS Positive and Negative Affect Schedule — momentary emotional state in ~5 minutes Self-Report How It Works 20 items (10 positive, 10 negative emotions) rated on 5-point Likert scale (1=not at all, 5=extremely). Administered at single timepoint or repeated across sessions. Temporal Dynamics Captures state affect at moment of administration (~5 min survey). Sensitive to within-session changes if administered pre/post intervention. Strengths Quick administration (≤5 min) — low participant burden Psychometrically robust (Watson, Clark, & Tellegen 1988) — widely used across studies Separates valence cleanly (positive vs. negative) Validated in 30+ languages Weaknesses Demand characteristics — participants guess the study hypothesis Cannot capture unconscious/implicit emotional responses Retrospective bias — relies on memory and introspection Floor/ceiling effects in extreme emotional states Theory Connections Arousal theory (captures valence dimension). This taxonomy helps you navigate 14 paradigm families by matching them to your research goal, resources, and constraints. Methods by Question Type Strengths & Weaknesses Method-Hypothesis Matcher Level 1: "What happens?" (Descriptive/Exploratory) Establishing phenomena, estimating effect sizes, and understanding ecological validity Use when: You need baseline data, population estimates, or real-world behavior patterns POE Post-Occupancy Evaluation Surveys of existing building occupants about their actual experiences and satisfaction. Strengths High ecological validity; real occupants in real spaces Can measure long-term adaptation and satisfaction Builds direct relationships with practitioners Limitations No control over design variables; confounded naturally Retrospective bias and self-report measurement error Cannot infer causation from correlations VR: Low L1 Example Question Are office workers satisfied with open-plan seating arrangements? headings: How Theories Generate Experiments at Each Level
Practical Guide: Upgrading Your Experiment's Level
Where Theories Diverge: Opportunities for Critical Experiments
Prospect-Refuge vs. Content-Based Theories (ART, SRT, Biophilia)
Why Theory Matters for Experimental Design
Severity: The Measure of Experimental Value
What This Means for Your Experiments
pages.excerpt: Theory & Experiment Design — COGS 160 COGS 160 Knowledge Navigator Theory & Design Hypothesis Builder Methods Taxonomy Measurement Instruments Sensor Catalogue Epistemic Network Bayesian Network Experiment Builder System Inventory Theory and Experiment Design Not all experiments are created equal. A study that merely asks "do plants improve mood?" differs fundamentally from one that asks "does the attention-restoration mechanism predicted by Kaplan's ART actually operate through involuntary attention, and can we distinguish its effects from those of stress recovery?" This module teaches the difference — and why it matters for the experiments you will design. The 4-Level Taxonomy Major Theories Theory → Experiment Critical Experiments Epistemological Foundations Environmental psychology experiments vary enormously in what they try to accomplish. A useful way to think about this variation is a hierarchy of theoretical ambition — from descriptive work that maps the territory to critical experiments that adjudicate between rival theoretical programmes. Each level builds on the previous ones, requires deeper theoretical understanding, and produces knowledge of a different kind. Deborah Mayo — Error statistician "The value of an experiment is not how surprising the result is, but how severely it tested the hypothesis. A severe test is one where, if the hypothesis were false, the experiment would very probably have detected the error." Severity increases as you move from Level 1 to

pages.title: Technical Setup — COGS 160 — Knowledge Atlas
headings: The Big Picture: How This Course Works
Using Context Files with Your AI Tool
pages.excerpt: Technical Setup — COGS 160 — Knowledge Atlas Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course Student explorer Researcher Contributor Instructor Practitioner Theory Explorer STUDENT EXPLORER COGS160 Start / Technical Setup COGS 160 · Technical Setup Guide Set Up Your Development Environment This page walks you through everything you need to start working on your track. No prior experience with AI tools, Git, or programming is assumed. Follow the steps in order — each one builds on the last. 0 The Big Picture: How This Course Works Understand the workflow before you install anything In COGS 160, you build real software tools that contribute to the Knowledge Atlas — a research system that organizes scientific evidence about how built environments affect human cognition and well-being. The key difference from a normal programming class is that you work with an AI coding assistant, not alone. The workflow is: you think , the AI writes code , and you verify and improve . You are the scientist-designer; the AI is the fast typist. Your job is to define what to build, evaluate whether it works, and fix what goes wrong. You are not expected to be a programmer. Many students in this course have never written code before. That is fine. The AI tools handle the syntax; you handle the thinking. What you need is curiosity, willingness to experiment, and the ability to describe what you want clearly. Here is what each piece of the system does: The Knowledge Atlas Website This

pages.title: Track TWO: AI Article Pipeline — COGS 160
pages.excerpt: Track TWO: AI Article Pipeline — COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course Student explorer Researcher Contributor Instructor Practitioner Theory Explorer STUDENT EXPLORER COGS 160 · Track 2 of 4 Track TWO: AI Article Pipeline Build automated article discovery systems using AI. Implement RAG-based retrieval, relevance scoring, and Value of Information prioritization to continuously find and filter research articles. Track Overview This track is not about manually finding more citations. It's about automating the article discovery process using artificial intelligence and information theory. The Goal: Build a system that continuously hunts for relevant papers and presents them to other students ranked by how much they'd contribute to filling knowledge gaps. You'll combine Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), relevance filtering, and Value of Information (VOI) scoring. Core Skills You'll Develop Automated Retrieval: Query engines that connect to Semantic Scholar API and other open-access sources RAG Systems: Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipelines that rank articles by relevance to existing knowledge Relevance Filtering: Distinguish experimental articles from theory papers and meta-reviews Value of Information: Prioritize which articles would most advance the knowledge base Quality Assurance: Automated gates that verify article type, topic relevance, and data quality Benchmarking: Test and measure retrieval accuracy against human-selected artic

pages.title: Track THREE: VR for Experiments — COGS 160
headings: Why VR for Built Environment Research?
pages.excerpt: Track THREE: VR for Experiments — COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course Student explorer Researcher Contributor Instructor Practitioner Theory Explorer STUDENT EXPLORER COGS 160 · Track 3 of 4 Track THREE: VR for Experiments Create a library of configurable VR environments for controlled experiments. Build parametric scenes where ceiling height, lighting, materials, and other variables can be adjusted to match real experimental needs. Track Overview Built environments constrain what experiments are possible. But running experiments in real buildings is costly and slow. Your track creates a collection of reusable VR environments that experimentalists can customize to run the studies they need. The Goal: Build a library of configurable VR environments that can be rapidly modified to match experimental protocols. Each environment should be packaged as a reusable template with clear documentation so researchers can run controlled studies in virtual scenes. Why VR for Built Environment Research? Control: Real buildings have hundreds of confounding variables. VR lets you isolate individual factors (lighting, layout, materials) that would be impossible to control in the field. Replicability: Run the exact same environment across multiple subjects. Impossible in the real world. Scale: Test dozens of environment variations quickly and cheaply. A real building takes months and millions of dollars. Measurement: Integrate eye tracking, physiological se

pages.title: Track FOUR: Interaction Design & Evaluation — COGS 160
pages.excerpt: Track FOUR: Interaction Design & Evaluation — COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course Student explorer Researcher Contributor Instructor Practitioner Theory Explorer STUDENT EXPLORER COGS 160 · Track 4 of 4 Track FOUR: Interaction Design & Evaluation Understand how different user types navigate Knowledge Atlas. Analyze their information needs, improve interaction design, and measure whether the system actually works for them. Track Overview Knowledge Atlas is complex. We need to personalize it for different user types because a student explorer's journey is very different from an instructor's, or a researcher's, or a theory explorer's. Your track is about understanding those differences and making the system better for each user type. The Goal: Map out how different user types navigate the K-Atlas system. Identify missing CTAs, dead ends, information overload, and misalignments between user needs and page structure. Then test improvements with real users and measure success. User Types We Support Student Explorer Learning the field. Wants overview of topics, propositions, evidence. Navigates by curiosity. Researcher Testing hypotheses. Needs deep access to evidence, methodologies, caveats. Searches by topic/methodology. Contributor Adding to K-Atlas. Needs to understand schema, propose new warrants, upload evidence. Oriented toward creation. Instructor Teaching with K-Atlas. Needs to understand propositions deeply, curate content for courses,

pages.excerpt: Week 7 — Sprint 2 Demo & Integration · COGS 160 K-ATLAS | Schedule Setup Topics Evidence Ask COGS 160 · Spring 2026 COGS 160 · Spring 2026 · Week 7 of 8 Sprint 2 Demo & Integration Present Sprint 2. Begin connecting the pieces across tracks. Tuesday: Cross-track Integration Thursday: Sprint 2 Demos Methodology Log #4 Tuesday · Day 13 · Integration Testing Integration Testing Across Tracks Knowledge Atlas is not four independent tracks. It is a unified system where each track's output feeds into another track's input. The Image Tagger produces tagged images. The Article Finder produces vetted articles. The VR Production track converts articles into spatial experiences. The GUI Evaluation track measures how well the interface serves users. Today you discover whether these pieces actually fit together. 1 Cross-Track Integration Test Each track's Sprint 2 output feeds into at least one other track. You will perform the integration test: does Track 2's article data feed correctly into Track 1's tagging interface? Does Track 4's GUI correctly display Track 1's tagged images? Does the data format produced by one track match the data format expected by the next track? If not, who needs to change their code — the producer or the consumer? This is where you discover contract misalignment. 2 Fix Integration Failures The most common problem at integration boundaries is vocabulary mismatch. pages.title: Crosswalk GUI mockups — design exploration
headings: Crosswalk GUI mockups — a design exploration
pages.excerpt: Crosswalk GUI mockups — design exploration Crosswalk GUI mockups — a design exploration 2026-04-18 · Working mockup over Codex's topic_crosswalk.json (102 defended rows × 18 outcomes × 9 architectural families). Four GUI patterns, same underlying data, rendered in-browser so the affordances can be felt before we commit to one. This page is a design artefact, not production UI. What the crosswalk is and what it is not. Per docs/TOPIC_CROSSWALK_AND_TOPIC_PAGE_CONTRACT_2026-04-19.md , the crosswalk is a bridge layer between human-language outcomes (stress, attention, restoration, …), architectural families (luminous, acoustic, spatial, …), and defended Knowledge Atlas topic bundles. One row = one outcome × one architectural family × one topic bundle × its linked papers, theories, measures. It is not a rival taxonomy; it is the join surface that makes the taxonomies intelligible to a user who says "show me architecture and stress." The patterns below differ only in how they let a user travel across that surface. 1. Heatmap matrix (Tufte / Bertin) The crosswalk's two root dimensions (architectural family × outcome) rendered as a 9 × 18 grid. Each cell's depth of colour encodes paper_count ; empty cells are left grey. Clicking a cell drills into its defended bundles. The view shows at a glance where the evidence base is dense ( luminous × cog.attention , natural × affect.restoration ) and where it is thin. paper count scale: 0 low mid high Best for: honest overview — users see cove

pages.title: About — Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: About — Knowledge Atlas On this page What it is Who it serves Epistemics How it's built People Get involved About the Knowledge Atlas A working research atlas joining cognitive neuroscience to environmental and architectural design. Maintained by Prof. David Kirsh and the COGS 160 Spring 2026 collaborators at UC San Diego. What the Atlas is The Knowledge Atlas indexes peer-reviewed work in environmental-cognition research, organises it under eleven foundational neuroscience frameworks, and maps each finding to testable architectural implications. It is a living document, regenerated on a weekly cycle, that is as honest about what the field does not yet know as it is about what it does. The Atlas covers three intertwined sub-fields: cognitive neuroscience — how perception, attention, affect, and memory actually work in the brain; environmental psychology — how built and natural environments shape human behaviour and well-being; and evidence-grounded design — how to translate both into practice that can be measured and falsified rather than merely asserted. 906 Articles 71 Neural mechanisms 11 T1 frameworks 13 T1.5 theories Who the Atlas serves Four audiences share the site, each with a slightly different navigation path. Researchers use it to scan topics, evidence, and gaps for scholarly work. Practitioners — architects, landscape designers, building scientists — use it to find evidence that bears on a specific design decision. Contributors add articles, tag images, and help r

pages.title: Agent directory — Knowledge Atlas
headings: Structured roles for writing, charting, audit, and design
What this page is — and what it isn't
pages.excerpt: Agent directory — Knowledge Atlas Knowledge Atlas agents Structured roles for writing, charting, audit, and design A K-ATLAS agent is a structured role with a citation-grounded reference group, a one-sentence mission, a required-output pack, and a checklist of things it must never do. Agents are meant to be run as sub-prompts by you — whether as a student preparing a Track 4 deliverable, a contributor writing a page's copy, or a reviewer auditing a design proposal. The full agent source lives under /agents/ in the repo; this page is the human-readable index. What this page is — and what it isn't This page is a directory, not an execution surface. Agents are prompt contracts — named roles, reference groups, required outputs — that run in an LLM session on the backend (Claude, GPT, Gemini, or a local model). There is no "Run this agent" button on this page. The agents work by being read into a sub-prompt, not by being clicked. How to invoke an agent Open the agent's source ( /agents/*.md ) to review its reference group, required inputs, and required outputs. Copy the "Prompt to run this agent" block at the bottom of the source. Paste into a Claude / GPT / Gemini session — Cowork, Claude Code, the web client, or a backend service with an API key — with the specific page, chart, or draft attached as context. The LLM returns the structured report the agent specifies. Apply the findings; re-run the agent on the revised draft if you want a second pass. A browser-invocation surface (

pages.excerpt: AI-Directed Development: The KA Methodology Knowledge At las Explore Topic Map Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute COGS160 Start > AI Methodology COGS 160 · AI-Directed Development AI-Directed Development*: The KA Methodology In this course you use AI as a directed instrument in a production pipeline — not as a chatbot. The goal is not "getting good output." It is producing output that meets a formal specification, passes defined tests, and integrates correctly with the larger system. That requires a specific methodology. CORE The central distinction: chatbot vs. directed instrument Why the standard way of using AI fails in production systems When you use an AI chatbot casually, the implicit contract is: "give me something useful and I will decide whether to use it." This works for one-off tasks where you can evaluate quality by reading the output. It fails completely when the output must satisfy a formal specification, integrate with other code, and be correct enough that downstream processes do not need to check every claim. In Knowledge Atlas, an AI extracting claims from a scientific paper is part of a 12-stage pipeline. If it invents a citation that does not exist, that fabrication propagates through the epistemic network, inflates credence scores, and corrupts the Bayesian network downstream. By the time a human notices, ten derivative claims may have been generated from the fabricated source. There is no casual recovery. The methodology in this document treats AI as a di

pages.title: How AI Systems Actually Fail — Knowledge Atlas
headings: Failures that emerge over long sessions
Failures that affect teams and codebases
Quick reference: all eleven failure modes
pages.excerpt: How AI Systems Actually Fail — Knowledge Atlas K-ATLAS AI Methodology Schedule Site Map COGS 160 · Lessons from the field How AI Systems Actually Fail These are not hypothetical risks. Every failure mode on this page happened to us while building Knowledge Atlas, or has been documented by other teams building production systems with AI. The patterns are predictable. The damage is preventable — but only if you know what to watch for. Knowledge Atlas was built with three AI systems working in parallel — Claude, Gemini, and Codex — across hundreds of sessions over months. The system grew from an empty repo to ~1,900 evidence claims, ~4,810 edges, a seven-type warrant taxonomy, and dozens of pipeline scripts. Every failure mode below was discovered the hard way: by losing time, losing data, or producing output that looked correct and was not. The first five failures (F1–F5) are prompt-level : they emerge in a single AI interaction. The next four (F6–F9) are session-level : they emerge over long sessions (30+ prompts) and compound silently. The final two (F10–F11) are systemic : they affect how entire teams and codebases degrade when AI is used at scale. PROMPT-LEVEL Failures within a single interaction These emerge immediately. A well-structured prompt prevents them. F1 Plausible Hallucination It reads like a fact. It is fabricated. The AI generates content that uses correct domain vocabulary, follows proper citation format, and is entirely invented. Fake citations with real-soun

pages.excerpt: Annotation Inspector · Knowledge Atlas Knowledge At las Explore Topic Map Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Epistemic Layer Annotation Layer Browse and manage annotations across the belief network. Filter by type and target belief, add new annotations (admin only), and explore the annotation taxonomy. 40 annotations 33 targets 6 active types 57 total beliefs About Annotation Types CALIBRATION_NOTE Credence flagged for manual review — too high or too low relative to evidential base SENSITIVITY_FLAG Belief credence is highly sensitive to network topology changes REPLICATION_STATUS Cross-paper replication count for this finding SURPRISE_FLAG Credence significantly above or below its theory's mean DISPUTE Contradicted by one or more other beliefs in the web CROSS_DOMAIN Belief bridges multiple theories — high interdisciplinary relevance Proposed New Types (Future) CONFOUND_DETECTED — A third variable may explain co-occurrence of findings; confound warning MECHANISM_GAP — A mechanistic link is hypothesized but lacks direct evidence SCOPE_LIMITATION — Finding is population- or context-specific; generalization risk THEORY_CONFLICT — Finding is inconsistent with predictions of a named framework theory REPLICATION_NEEDED — Finding appears only once in corpus; urgently needs replication Select an annotation to view details Annotation Inspector • Knowledge Atlas COGS 160 • March 2026 Data source: ka_payloads/annotations.json • Questions: dkirsh@gmail.com KA UI · v0.5 · Annotations ← Bac

pages.title: EN Warrants — Plain-English Breakdown
headings: The seven warrants, in plain English
Coherence with the rest of the corpus
pages.excerpt: EN Warrants — Plain-English Breakdown 6.4 71 st percentile Why this article scores 6.4 Boyce, Hunter & Howlett (2003) · paper_id PDF-0011 EN 6.4 means this article is judged more credible than 71 % of the 760 articles currently in the K-ATLAS corpus. The percentile is computed against the live distribution of EN scores; it shifts as the corpus grows. The Evidence Number (EN) is not a single judgment. It is the combination of seven typed warrants, each contributing a credibility weight ( d ) between 0.25 and 1.0. The weights below describe what each warrant adds (or subtracts) for this specific article. The full warrant taxonomy is documented in contracts/web_of_belief.md ; the per-article values are persisted at pipeline stage 17 ( warrant_assignment ). The seven warrants, in plain English Source warrant Source quality Where was it published, and who reviewed it? This article is a sponsored research-centre report, not a peer-reviewed journal article. Credible source, but not the strongest tier. d = 0.65 moderate Method warrant Method quality Is the design appropriate to the claim? The paper is a structured narrative review of empirical evidence rather than a primary study or a formal meta-analysis — strong for synthesis, weaker for causal precision. d = 0.55 moderate Statistical warrant Statistical strength How large and how stable is the headline effect? The cited studies report effect sizes around d = 0.16 with sample n = 22 in some cases — small effects, modest power, but

pages.excerpt: Understanding the System — K-ATLAS Knowledge At las Explore Topic Map Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Author: K-ATLAS Science-Writing Agent Date: 2026-03-27 Version: Living Document — update as system evolves Atlas documentation index → Chapter 1 What K-ATLAS Is K-ATLAS — the Knowledge Architecture for Translating Life-science and Affective-environment Science — is a reasoning machine built around a single conviction: that a scientific claim is only as strong as the evidence and argumentation that support it. Where a conventional database stores facts, K-ATLAS stores structured credences : beliefs tagged with how well-supported they are, where the support comes from, and why the system holds each belief with the confidence it does. The closest popular analogy is Google's PageRank. PageRank scores web pages not by counting how many links point to them, but by weighing the authority of those linking pages, recursively. K-ATLAS does something analogous for scientific beliefs: a claim is not simply "in the literature" — it receives a credence value that reflects the strength, independence, and theoretical warrant of the evidence behind it. When new evidence arrives, credences propagate through the network, and the whole system re-equilibrates, much as PageRank scores shift when new pages are indexed. Example — PageRank Analogy Suppose three independent studies show that forest environments reduce cortisol. Each study is a "link" pointing to the belief Forest exposure lowers cor

A first-pass explainer; factual claims cross-checked against the literature, but the synthesis is still being refined by a domain panel. Use as a starting point, not the final word. Reviewers: annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1 · Foundational Framework Chronobiological Regulation Light, time, and temperature coordinate every physiological function on a roughly 24-hour schedule. When the schedule is violated, alertness, mood, metabolism, and cognition all pay the cost. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. Chronobiological Regulation is the framework that treats the brain and body as a coupled oscillator system running on a roughly 24-hour cycle. Nearly every tissue keeps its own local clock, and a master clock in the hypothalamus synchronises the rest — mostly through the lighting environment humans experienced for the past few hundred thousand years. Physiologically, the master clock is the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus, a pair of small nuclei of about 20,000 neurons each. Intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs), discovered in 2002, express melanopsin and project directly via the retinohypothalamic tract to the SCN, carrying the light signal that entrains the clock. The SCN drives a downstream cascade: through the pineal gland it controls melatonin (h

T1 · Foundational Framework Default-mode & Place Place cells, grid cells, and the default-mode network together form the brain's machinery for knowing where you are, where you have been, and where you might go — in physical space and in the mental space of memory and imagination. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. Default-mode & Place names the joint contribution of two intimately linked systems: the hippocampal-entorhinal "place-coding" circuit that builds maps of physical space, and the cortical default-mode network that reuses that machinery for memory, daydreaming, and imagination. Knowing where you are and knowing what happened to you last Tuesday turn out to share substrate. Physiologically, the place system is now one of the best-understood circuits in the brain. Hippocampal place cells fire when the animal occupies a specific location; entorhinal grid cells tile space with a hexagonal lattice; boundary cells in the subiculum and parahippocampal region fire at walls and doorways; head-direction cells in the anterior thalamus and retrosplenial cortex encode facing direc

T1 · Foundational Framework Dual-Task / Attentional Load The framework that treats attention as a limited metabolic resource, explains why doing two things at once degrades both, and shows why mental effort feels aversive in a way that physical effort often does not. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. Attention is not a light that shines equally across every task; it is a limited resource that has to be divided when demands overlap, and the division is rarely clean. Dual-Task / Attentional Load describes how the brain allocates this resource, what happens when it runs out, and why "just concentrate" is a cognitive request with a real metabolic price. Physiologically, the framework rests on three documented features. First, working memory has a hard capacity ceiling of roughly four items (Cowan) implemented in a fronto-parietal network — dorsolateral prefrontal cortex for maintenance, parietal cortex for attentional priority, basal ganglia for gating. Second, selective attention modulates sensory-cortex activity up and down via top-down projections carrying alpha- and

A first-pass explainer; factual claims cross-checked against the literature, but the synthesis is still being refined by a domain panel. Use as a starting point, not the final word. Reviewers: annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1 · Foundational Framework Embodied Cognition Cognition is not a disembodied computation that happens in the brain — it uses the body and the environment as active components. The built environment is therefore not a container for thinking but a partner in it. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. Embodied Cognition is the claim that thinking is not confined to a brain processing abstract symbols but is constitutively shaped by the body's sensorimotor capacities and by the environment within which the body moves. We perceive a staircase as climbable, a doorway as passable, a chair as sit-on-able — and those perceptions are not inferences made after the fact; they are how perception happens. Physiologically, the framework is supported by four converging observations. Premotor cortex and parietal multimodal areas activate during the perception of action-relevant objects, not only during action itself — a body of evidence initiated by the mirror neuron work of Rizzolatti and Gallese in the 1990s. T1 · Foundational Framework Interoceptive-Constructionist Affect Emotion is not a read-out from dedicated "emotion centres" but the brain's ongoing inference about the state of the body, assembled from bodily signals and context. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. The Interoceptive-Constructionist account of affect holds that emotions are not hardwired responses triggered by fixed stimuli but constructed inferences the brain makes about what its body is doing and why — a view that joins William James's original insight to modern predictive-processing neuroscience. Physiologically, three streams converge. Vagal afferents carry heart-rate, respiration, and visceral signals from the nucleus of the solitary tract up through the parabrachial nucleus and thalamus to the posterior, mid-, and anterior insula, which together build a progressively more integrated representation of bodily state. Anterior insula (AI) generates interoceptive predictions and compares them to the actual interoceptive signal, producing interoceptive prediction error. Parallel input from the H

T1 · Superordinate Configurer Implicit-Explicit Dual-Process Theory The meta-framework that decides when the other ten Tier-1 systems run in fast automatic mode versus slow deliberate mode. Not a mechanism in its own right but a controller that configures the ones below. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. The brain runs two qualitatively different kinds of processing in parallel and decides — moment by moment — which one is in control of a given task. Implicit processing is fast, automatic, parallel, and effortless; explicit processing is slow, deliberate, serial, and tiring. IE_DPT is the meta-framework that describes the switching rule between them. Physiologically, dual-process architecture maps onto two large-scale networks. Implicit/automatic processing draws on well-learned circuits distributed across sensory and motor cortices, striatum, amygdala, and cerebellum — regions that have consolidated a skill to the point of procedural efficiency. Explicit/deliberate processing recruits the cognitive control network: dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, dorsal

Reviewers: annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1 · Foundational Framework Multisensory Integration The brain reconciles sight, sound, touch, smell, and proprioception into a single coherent experience — and flags ambiguity when the senses disagree. Coherent environments feel solid; conflicting ones feel subtly off. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. Multisensory Integration is the process by which the brain combines information from the separate senses into a unified perceptual experience, weighting each modality by how reliable it is in the current context. When signals agree, perception sharpens; when they disagree subtly, the disagreement registers as unease. Physiologically, integration happens at multiple levels. The superior colliculus in the midbrain contains deep-layer neurons that respond to spatially and temporally aligned stimuli from different modalities with superadditive enhancement — a response larger than the sum of unimodal responses, and confined to a specific "temporal binding window" typically 100–300 ms wide. Cortical multisensory regions (superior temporal sulcus, posterior parietal cortex, insu

A first-pass explainer; factual claims cross-checked against the literature, but the synthesis is still being refined by a domain panel. Use as a starting point, not the final word. Reviewers: annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1 · Foundational Framework Motor-Sensory Integration Every movement the body makes is predicted before it happens, and the prediction is used to interpret the sensory feedback that follows. Perception and action are one closed loop, not two processes. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. When the brain sends a motor command, it simultaneously sends a copy of that command ("efference copy") to its own sensory regions so they can predict the sensory consequences of the action before they arrive. This is why you cannot tickle yourself — your motor system predicted the touch and the sensory system down-weighted it. Physiologically, the loop rests on a suite of well-characterised circuits. Premotor and primary motor cortices issue commands and send copies to posterior parietal cortex and cerebellum. The cerebellum maintains internal models of body dynamics — a forward model that predicts sensory consequences, and an inverse model that plans motor commands to achieve desired sensory outcomes. Reviewers: annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1 · Foundational Framework Neuromodulatory Systems The slow-acting chemical signals — dopamine, noradrenaline, acetylcholine, serotonin — that set the gain on everything else the brain does. Arousal, reward, vigilance, and restoration are all their work. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. Neuromodulatory systems are the small clusters of neurons — a few tens of thousands of cells in a brain of a hundred billion — whose axons fan out across the entire cortex and reset the operating parameters of everything they touch. They do not carry content; they carry state . Four systems do most of the work in environmental cognition. Dopamine , arising from the ventral tegmental area (VTA) and substantia nigra (SN), signals reward prediction error and motivates approach. Noradrenaline , from the locus coeruleus (LC), tracks unexpected uncertainty and sets attentional gain. Acetylcholine , from the basal forebrain and pedunculopontine nucleus, gates cortical attention via muscarinic and nicotinic receptors. Read it as a useful starting point, not as the final word. Reviewers: annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1 · Foundational Framework Predictive Processing The brain as a prediction machine: how anticipation, surprise, and precision-weighted error together build what we perceive, attend to, and learn. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. Predictive Processing is the idea that perception is not the passive registration of sensory signals but an ongoing inferential act — the brain constantly generates predictions about what it is about to encounter and processes mainly the residual "prediction error" its predictions failed to anticipate. The physiology is now partially mapped. Deep cortical layers 5 and 6 send top-down predictions via slow alpha and beta-band oscillations through NMDA-receptor-dependent pyramidal projections; superficial layers 2 and 3 return prediction errors via fast gamma-band signals through AMPA-receptor-dependent feedforward connections. pages.excerpt: Salience Networks (SN) · T1 Framework · Knowledge Atlas Working draft · expert review in progress. A first-pass explainer; factual claims cross-checked against the literature, but the synthesis is still being refined by a domain panel. Use as a starting point, not the final word. Reviewers: annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1 · Foundational Framework Salience Networks The system that decides what gets noticed, when the brain switches between inward reflection and outward task, and how threat, novelty, and behavioural relevance compete for attention. On this page At a glance (300 words) Deep dive (1,500 words) References At a glance In plain English. The Salience Network is the brain's relevance detector — a small coalition of regions, centred on the anterior insula and the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex, that tags incoming signals as either worth responding to or safe to ignore, and then hands control of attention to whichever system should act next. Three linked structures do most of the work. The anterior insula (AI) pools interoceptive, exteroceptive, and valuation signals; the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) monitors conflict and anticipated error; the temporoparietal junction (TPJ) pulls attention toward unexpected events. Together they function as a dynamic switch, toggling activity between the Default Mode Network (inward, self- referential processing) and the Central Executive Network (outward, task-focused processing). The point is to stop guessing, avoid duplicate accounts, and apply the smallest fix that restores the student's access. Open Manual Reset Tool Open Sign-In Page Open Registration Page First Ask for the exact email address and the exact error message or a screenshot. Second Do not create a second account unless you are sure the student never registered. Third If password recovery email is not working, use the manual reset link from the instructor review page. After Access Returns Send the student to Account Settings to change email or password properly. Most Common Student says: “I forgot my password” Use the manual reset tool. This is the default remedy when mail delivery is unreliable. Registration Student sees: “That email is not registered” Determine whether the account truly does not exist, or whether the student is using the wrong address. Duplicate Account Risk Student sees: “An account with that email already exists” Do not register again. Treat this as an existing-account recovery case. Not Password Student can sign in, but Question 1 or Question 2 is wrong or blank This is an assignment-state problem, not a password problem. The full deliverable chain lives at Track 4 hub, Region 3.5 . ◧ What this page displays the information on offer The argumentation layer would render Dung's (1995) abstract argumentation frameworks over the Atlas's claim corpus. Nodes are arguments — a claim plus its supporting reasons. Edges are attacks : one argument defeats another. The computation produces an acceptability labelling that sorts every argument into IN (accepted), OUT (rejected), or UNDEC (undecided), under a chosen semantics (grounded, preferred, or stable). Where the EN shows epistemic support, the argumentation layer shows argumentative structure — which argument defeats which, and which coherent sets of arguments survive mutual attack. It makes contested claims visible as contested, without pretending to resolve the contest. The page does not yet exist. What follows i

Edges come in four flavours: support , undercutting defeat , rebutting defeat , and coherence . The viewer answers one question well: given a claim you care about — say, "exposure to fractal patterns reduces self-reported stress" — which other claims in the Atlas support it, which undercut it, and how densely connected is the neighbourhood? A front is a topic + time-window combination where the number of high-quality papers indexed in the last, say, 18 months is high relative to the topic's historical baseline. Fronts are useful for orientation but treacherous for inference: a spike in publications is not evidence of truth, and a dip is not evidence of resolution. The page must present velocity as a navigation signal, not as an epistemic one. The page does not yet exist. Four elements: a short prose answer, a confidence band, the warrants cited, and an explicit disclosure of what the system won't answer because the corpus is silent or inconsistent. QA is the surface most vulnerable to LLM hallucination. The design problem is not just "return a helpful answer" but two stronger commitments: refuse to answer when the corpus does not support one, and never cite a warrant the retrieval step did not actually return. Where the evidence is dense — or absent
◈ Who owns these pages readable by all tracks; redesigned or implemented by one track each
ℹ︎ Reading the status labels what the badges mean
pages.excerpt: Complicated journey surfaces — Knowledge Atlas Complicated surfaces index The harder pages of Knowledge Atlas The Atlas has ordinary pages — a home, a topic browser, an article view — that most visitors can navigate without guidance. It also has a second class of pages that demand more of both the designer and the reader: epistemic and Bayesian network viewers, an interpretation layer, an argumentation layer, a question-answering surface, mechanism and theory deep-dives, gap and evidence-landscape maps, research-front trackers, and two structural inspectors. These pages exist because the underlying representation demands them — you cannot adequately show a web of belief in a simple list — but each one poses a design problem that ordinary page patterns do not solve. This index lists them and their current implementation status. Every linked page on this index follows the same scaffold: what it displays, who needs it, why the design is hard, the current naive solution, and a critique scaffold for each user type. If you are a Track 4 student, these are candidate T4.a / T4.c targets; if you are a researcher, they are where the site's interpretive claims live. Layer C · Network representations Belief & causal networks Graphs — nodes and edges. The first and most load-bearing design challenge on the site. Epistemic Network (Web of Belief) viewer naive Bayesian Network viewer naive Layer D · Reading evidence Interpretation, argument, QA How the Atlas talks about what it has indexed

pages.title: Mechanisms · Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: Mechanisms · Knowledge Atlas Mechanisms Proposed causal chains through which features of the environment produce cognitive, affective, or behavioural outcomes. Each entry names the chain and states its evidentiary status in one line. Noise → cortisol → cognitive performance Well-supported · open-plan offices, classrooms Chronic background noise above about 55 dBA sustains cortisol elevation, which produces a measurable decrement on hippocampal-dependent memory tasks. Daylight → circadian entrainment → alertness Well-supported · office and residential Morning exposure to at least 250 melanopic lux activates intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells, advancing the suprachiasmatic nucleus phase and producing daytime alertness gains. Ceiling height → construal level → creativity Partial support · Meyers-Levy & Zhu · 2007 High ceilings prime a "freedom" construct and raise construal level, producing modest creativity gains; original laboratory finding replicates inconsistently and field effects are small. Thermal comfort → affect → prosocial behaviour Speculative · Williams & Bargh · 2008 (challenged) Warmer ambient temperature was proposed to prime a "warmth" concept that shifts prosocial judgement; large multi-lab replication efforts have failed, so the mechanism is retained here as a cautionary example. Crowding → cognitive load → error rate Partial support · dense residential, healthcare Perceived social density imposes working-memory load from inhibiting social moni

Instead of scattering notes, articles, topics, evidence, and questions across separate pages, Atlas should let you gather them into folders, review them, and export them as a working dossier. The current workspace persists in the browser. That is already better than having no visible collection at all, but it is not yet server-backed. pages.title: PNU Elaboration · Knowledge Atlas
headings: Open work — what it would take to finish this PNU
pages.excerpt: PNU Elaboration · Knowledge Atlas Plausible Neural Underpinning — Common-Sense Walk-through For Boyce, Hunter & Howlett (2003), The Benefits of Daylight through Windows · paper_id: PDF-0011 · PNU id: PNU-DAYLIGHT-CIRCADIAN-001 The naked sequence This is the same compact chain shown on the article page. We restate it here so that the elaboration can hang directly off it. daylight at the eye → melanopsin-bearing retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) → suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) → locus coeruleus / pineal → cortical arousal & circadian phase → alertness, mood, sleep PNU metadata PNU id PNU-DAYLIGHT-CIRCADIAN-001 Proposed by K-ATLAS PNU panel · 2026 cycle (provisional record) Source articles Boyce, Hunter & Howlett (2003) · Berson et al. (2002) · Brainard et al. (2001) Pipeline stage 13 — pnu_generation Last reviewed (not yet panel-reviewed; auto-generated chain) Common-sense gloss Missing — to be authored. See "Open work" below. Step-by-step (draft) The walk-through below is a first-pass draft. It will be replaced once the PNU panel writes the canonical common-sense explanation. Step 1 · Input Daylight reaches the eye The relevant feature is not just brightness. It is the spectral content of daylight: a broad, blue-shifted spectrum that peaks near 480 nm. Electric lighting can match daylight on visible brightness but typically delivers a different spectrum, with much less power at 480 nm. Each claim shows the neural mechanism chain, bridge type, CCI confidence level, and identified gaps in our current understanding. Article Light Exposure and Circadian Sleep Stevens et al. (2024) Evidence-based mechanisms connecting light exposure timing to circadian entrainment and sleep consolidation. pages.title: Neuro Perspective — Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: Neuro Perspective — Knowledge Atlas Knowledge At las Explore Topic Map Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute COGS 160 · Spring 2026 The Neuro Perspective Every claim in the Knowledge Atlas rests on neural mechanisms — chains of brain events that explain how built environments shape cognition, affect, and physiology. Choose a topic below to see what the brain is actually doing. Did You Know? 🧠 Your brain judges a room in 130 milliseconds A fast magnocellular pathway extracts scene "gist" — proportions, lighting distribution, spatial depth — before you consciously see any detail. This 130ms snapshot frames everything you feel about the space afterward. Bar (2007), Neuron; Friston (2010), Nature Reviews Neuroscience 💡 Wrong light at night costs you 42 minutes of sleep Melanopsin-expressing retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) don't care about color — they measure light intensity. Exposure to >10 melanopic lux within 1 hour of bedtime suppresses melatonin and delays sleep onset by 30-50 minutes on average. Berson et al. (2002), Science; Foster (2021), Lifetime of Light 🏛️ Buildings literally become part of your brain Andy Clark's extended cognition thesis isn't metaphor. Clear wayfinding extends spatial memory; organized workspaces extend working memory. The brain treats reliable environmental structure as part of its own cognitive architecture. pages.title: Theories · Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: Theories · Knowledge Atlas Theories Knowledge Atlas organises theories of environmental cognition in two tiers. Tier 1 (T1) frameworks are the foundational, mostly domain-neutral mechanisms that underpin how people perceive and respond to their surroundings. Tier 1.5 (T1.5) domain theories apply two or more T1 frameworks to a specific architectural or environmental concern. Tier definitions and mappings follow THEORY_HIERARCHY_IMPLEMENTATION.md . Tier 1 — Foundational frameworks Eleven mostly domain-neutral mechanisms: ten cognitive / neural systems and one superordinate configurer that decides when each system is recruited. PP Predictive Processing Perception, attention, and affect are driven by prediction, prediction error, and precision-weighting; environmental structure shapes what the agent predicts and how confidently. In-depth entry → SN Salience Networks Environmental cues recruit orienting, task-switching, and threat / surprise-detection systems, producing rapid reorientation to novelty-relevant or safety-relevant features. In-depth entry → DP Default-mode & Place Place encoding, internally directed cognition, and spatial memory binding mediate environmental experience through hippocampal and default-mode circuitry. In-depth entry → DT Dual-Task / Attentional Load Environments influence cognitive load, attentional depletion, and the competition between concurrent task demands and restoration. In-depth entry → NM Neuromodulatory Systems Environmental stimulation modul

pages.title: Theory · Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: Theory · Knowledge Atlas Living page. This theory profile is auto-generated from the K-ATLAS extraction pipeline and will grow as the corpus expands. Sections marked TODO are queued for development — see the course roadmap . Attention Restoration Theory Also known as: ART · Kaplan's restorative-environment framework Directed attention is a finite cognitive resource that becomes fatigued with sustained use; environments rich in "soft fascination" (natural scenes, flowing water, rustling leaves) allow that resource to replenish without voluntary effort. Originator(s) Rachel & Stephen Kaplan Year introduced 1989 Domain Environmental Psychology / Cognitive Science In our corpus 31 articles Status Well-established, actively debated §1 Core claims The load-bearing propositions of the theory, each with an empirical-status tag. C1 — Directed-attention fatigue Sustained use of directed (voluntary) attention produces a measurable fatigue state that degrades performance on tasks requiring concentration, inhibition, and working-memory maintenance. Well-established C2 — Four properties of restorative environments Restoration occurs in environments that offer four properties: being away (psychological distance from the depleting task), extent (rich enough to sustain exploration), fascination (attention-holding features that do not require directed effort), and compatibility (fit between the environment and the person's inclinations). Well-established C3 — Nature as privileged source of sof

pages.title: Adaptive Thermal Comfort · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: Adaptive Thermal Comfort · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas Draft — panel review requested. This page is a first-pass sci-writer explainer for Adaptive Thermal Comfort , drafted 2026-04-17 for instructor and panel review before it becomes canonical on the site. Factual claims have been cross-checked against the cited literature. Panel reviewers: please annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1.5 · Domain Theory Adaptive Thermal Comfort de Dear & Brager's demonstration that occupants' preferred temperature tracks recent outdoor conditions via homeostatic, autonomic, and behavioural adjustment. Originators: Richard de Dear & Gail Brager · 1998 · Code: ADAPTIVE_THERMAL On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. Adaptive Thermal Comfort theory (de Dear & Brager, 1998) overturned the long-standing Fanger (1970) PMV/PPD assumption that a single optimal temperature exists, showing instead that in naturally ventilated buildings occupants' comfort temperature varies systematically with running-mean outdoor temperature. The mechanism has three components: physiological acclimatisation via Interoception (IC) and Neuromodulation (NM); psychological expectation, a form of distributed inference (IE_DPT); and behavioural adjustment (clothing, posture, window operation, fan use) closing the regulatory loop. The theory is now embedded in ASHRAE Standard 55 (2013 onward) and CEN EN 16798-1, and has driven the shift from sealed HVAC to operable-windo

pages.title: Attention Restoration Theory · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas
headings: Deep dive — the theory in full
pages.excerpt: Attention Restoration Theory · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas Draft — panel review requested. This page is a first-pass sci-writer explainer for Attention Restoration Theory , drafted 2026-04-17 for instructor and panel review before it becomes canonical on the site. Factual claims have been cross-checked against the cited literature. Panel reviewers: please annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1.5 · Domain Theory Attention Restoration Theory Nature's restorative effect on directed attention: the Kaplans' account of how soft fascination refills a limited cognitive resource. Originators: Rachel Kaplan & Stephen Kaplan · 1989 · Code: ART On this page At a glance Deep dive (1,500 words) T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. ART proposes that directed attention is a limited, fatiguable resource and that environments rich in "soft fascination" — natural settings with gently engaging stimuli, coherent extent, and a sense of being away — permit directed attention to recover. The theory has four operational components. Being away : psychological or physical distance from demands. Extent : a coherent environment substantial enough to engage the mind. Soft fascination : stimuli that hold involuntary attention effortlessly without demanding it (trees in wind, streams, drifting clouds). Compatibility : a fit between environmental affordances and the agent's purposes. Together they support recovery of the top-down attentional control functions that everyday u

pages.title: Biophilia Hypothesis · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: Biophilia Hypothesis · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas Draft — panel review requested. T1.5 · Domain Theory Biophilia Hypothesis Wilson's claim that humans carry an evolved affinity for natural life and life-like forms, eliciting embodied safety signals and positive appraisal. Originators: Edward O. Wilson, Stephen Kellert · 1984 · Code: BIOPHILIA On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. The biophilia hypothesis (Wilson, 1984; Kellert & Wilson, 1993) holds that humans possess an evolved, partly innate affinity for life, lifelike processes, and biomorphic form. Exposure to plants, water, other animals, and natural patterns elicits embodied safety signals and positive affect that cannot be fully accounted for by ART or SRT alone. The hypothesis sits at the intersection of Neuromodulation (NM), Interoception (IC), and Embodied Cognition (EC): biophilic stimuli modulate autonomic state, are processed through interoceptive routes, and their restorative effects depend on whole-body rather than purely visual engagement. Kellert's (2008) typology distinguishes direct, indirect, and symbolic biophilic experience and has shaped the applied design

T1.5 · Domain Theory BRECVEMA — Music and Aesthetic Emotion Juslin's eight-mechanism model of how music and aesthetic stimuli induce emotion: Brain stem, Rhythmic entrainment, Evaluative conditioning, Contagion, Visual imagery, Episodic memory, Musical expectancy, Aesthetic judgment. Originators: Patrik Juslin · 2013 · Code: BRECVEMA On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. BRECVEMA (Juslin, 2013) is the most comprehensive contemporary account of the mechanisms by which music, and by extension other aesthetic stimuli, induce emotion. The eight mechanisms are: B rain stem reflex (loud sudden sounds), R hythmic entrainment (synchronisation of motor and autonomic rhythms), E valuative conditioning (learned pairings with affective events), C ontagion (mimicry of perceived emotional expression), V isual imagery (triggered scenes and bodily simulation), E pisodic memory (autobiographical association), M usical expectancy (prediction violation and resolution), and A esthetic judgment (deliberate evaluation). The model maps

pages.excerpt: Flow Theory · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas Draft — panel review requested. T1.5 · Domain Theory Flow Theory Csikszentmihalyi's account of optimal experience: the absorbed, autotelic state that emerges when challenge and skill are both high and balanced. Originators: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi · 1975 · Code: FLOW_THEORY On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. Flow Theory (Csikszentmihalyi, 1975, 1990) describes an absorbed, autotelic state characterised by focused concentration, merging of action and awareness, loss of self-consciousness, time-distortion, and intrinsic reward. The canonical antecedent is the challenge-skill balance: at high challenge with matched high skill, flow is reliably induced; mismatched challenge and skill produce anxiety (too hard) or boredom (too easy). The theory maps onto Salience Networks (SN) because flow involves attenuated self-referential salience; onto Decision Theory (DT) because the agent's value function is reweighted toward the activity itself; and onto Multisensory Integration (MSI) because flow states show increased binding of perceptual and motor streams. pages.excerpt: Goldilocks Principle · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas Draft — panel review requested. T1.5 · Domain Theory Goldilocks Principle Kidd, Piantadosi, & Aslin's account of attention allocation: organisms preferentially attend to stimuli of intermediate complexity, not the simplest or most complex available. Originators: Celeste Kidd, Steven Piantadosi & Richard Aslin · 2012 · Code: GOLDILOCKS_PRINCIPLE On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. The Goldilocks effect (Kidd, Piantadosi, & Aslin, 2012, 2014) describes the systematic preference organisms show for stimuli of intermediate complexity over stimuli that are either too simple or too complex. Infants look longer at events of moderate surprise than at highly predictable or highly unpredictable events, and this attentional bias generalises to adults and to non-human animals. The principle sits at the intersection of Predictive Processing (PP) — because "intermediate complexity" is well characterised as a moderate level of prediction error; Salience Networks (SN) — because the attentional effect is mediated by salience weighting; and Decision Theory (DT) — because the preference is interpr

pages.title: Kaplan Preference Matrix · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: Kaplan Preference Matrix · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas Draft — panel review requested. This page is a first-pass sci-writer explainer for Kaplan Preference Matrix , drafted 2026-04-17 for instructor and panel review before it becomes canonical on the site. Factual claims have been cross-checked against the cited literature. Panel reviewers: please annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1.5 · Domain Theory Kaplan Preference Matrix The Kaplans' four-factor model: environmental preference arises from a balance of coherence, complexity, legibility, and mystery. Originators: Rachel Kaplan & Stephen Kaplan · 1982 · Code: KAPLAN_PREFERENCE On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. The Kaplan Preference Matrix (Kaplan & Kaplan, 1982) proposes that environmental preference is well-predicted by four informational variables crossed on two dimensions: making sense (coherence, legibility) versus exploration (complexity, mystery), with each pair crossed by immediate versus inferred understanding. Coherence is the immediate organisation of a scene; legibility is the inferred promise that the scene would remain understandable as one moves through it; complexity is the immediate richness of information; mystery is the inferred promise of more information available on further exploration. The matrix has been empirically productive: meta-analytic work (Stamps, 2004) confirms moderate-to-large effects for each factor on preference ratings, thou

pages.excerpt: Place Attachment · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas Draft — panel review requested.
