T1.5 · Domain Theory Place Attachment Altman, Low, and Scannell's account of affective bonds between people and specific places — identity, dependence, and social dimensions. Originators: Irwin Altman, Setha Low, Leila Scannell & Robert Gifford · 1992 · Code: PLACE_ATTACHMENT On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. Place Attachment refers to the affective, cognitive, and behavioural bonds people form with specific places. The modern synthesis (Scannell & Gifford, 2010) organises the construct as a three-dimensional framework: person (individual vs. group attachment), process (affect, cognition, behaviour), and place (social vs. physical features, and scale). Two widely-used sub-constructs are place identity (Proshansky, 1978 — a place as part of self-definition) and place dependence (Stokols & Shumaker, 1981 — functional fit between a place and an agent's goals). Attachment predicts pro-environmental behaviour, conservation advocacy, and resistance to relocation. Mechanism intersects with Interoception (IC) because embodied safety signals accumulate with rep

T1.5 · Domain Theory Privacy Regulation Theory Altman's dialectical account of privacy as dynamic regulation of interpersonal access, achieved through spatial, behavioural, and verbal mechanisms. Originators: Irwin Altman · 1975 · Code: PRIVACY_REGULATION On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. Altman's (1975) Privacy Regulation Theory reframes privacy away from the common-sense notion of withdrawal and toward a dialectical dynamic: agents continuously regulate a desired versus achieved level of interaction, using spatial (distance, barriers), behavioural (gaze aversion, posture), verbal (topic shifts, silence), and territorial mechanisms. When achieved privacy matches desired privacy, the regulation is successful; mismatch causes stress, crowding, or isolation depending on direction. The theory has been load-bearing for open-plan office research, social media design, and information-privacy policy (Petronio, 2002, extended it as Communication Privacy Management). It intersects with Inferential Extended Distributed Processing Theory (IE_DPT

pages.title: Prospect-Refuge Theory · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas
pages.excerpt: Prospect-Refuge Theory · T1.5 Domain Theory · Knowledge Atlas Draft — panel review requested. This page is a first-pass sci-writer explainer for Prospect-Refuge Theory , drafted 2026-04-17 for instructor and panel review before it becomes canonical on the site. Factual claims have been cross-checked against the cited literature. Panel reviewers: please annotate inline and return to the instructor. T1.5 · Domain Theory Prospect-Refuge Theory Appleton's evolutionary account of environmental preference: we prefer settings that afford broad outward view (prospect) and sheltered vantage (refuge). Originators: Jay Appleton · 1975 · Code: PROSPECT_REFUGE On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. Prospect-Refuge Theory (Appleton, 1975) posits an evolved preference for environments that afford both unobstructed outward view (prospect, enabling threat detection and resource sighting) and sheltered vantage (refuge, affording protection from exposure). The combination — view without being viewed — is hypothesised to have conferred survival advantages and to generate the aesthetic responses we now report as "liking" a view. The theory grounds canonical design patterns: edge seating in restaurants, "cockpit" offices, courtyards within larger landscapes. Evidence has accumulated primarily through preference studies using photographs and VR, with converging results: a meta-analytic synthesis (Stamps, 2014) found robust effects on preference ratings but note

T1.5 · Domain Theory Soundscape Theory Schafer's and successors' framework for auditory environments as perceived, interpreted, and culturally situated wholes, not sums of sound sources. Originators: R. Murray Schafer · 1977 · Code: SOUNDSCAPE On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. Soundscape theory (Schafer, 1977; Truax, 1984) treats the acoustic environment as a perceptually and culturally constituted whole rather than a sum of measurable sound-pressure levels. Key moves: distinguishing keynote sounds (persistent background), sound signals (attended foreground events), and soundmarks (culturally distinctive sounds); classifying soundscapes by affective-qualitative dimensions rather than decibels alone (pleasantness × eventfulness; ISO 12913-1:2014); and emphasising context-dependent appraisal. Mechanistically: Predictive Processing (PP) because soundscape perception depends on auditory prediction error; Interoception (IC) because affective evaluation of sound recruits visceral pathways; Cross-modal Binding (CB) because soundscape perception is strongly

T1.5 · Domain Theory Space Syntax Hillier & Hanson's theory that spatial configuration — not individual rooms — drives movement, encounter, and cognition of built environments. Originators: Bill Hillier & Julienne Hanson · 1984 · Code: SPACE_SYNTAX On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. Space Syntax (Hillier & Hanson, 1984) is a set of mathematical techniques for analysing the topology of spatial configurations — streets, room adjacencies, sight-lines — and relating configurational properties (integration, choice, depth, connectivity) to movement patterns, co-presence, and emergent social use. The core empirical finding, replicated across hundreds of studies, is that movement density in urban and architectural networks is strongly predicted by configurational integration independent of local land-use, controlling for common confounds (Penn, 2003). The theory intersects with Predictive Processing (PP) because spatial configuration shapes prediction about where movement will occur; with Embodied Cognition (EC) because the analysis is grounded in bodily traversal rath

T1.5 · Domain Theory Stress Reduction Theory Ulrich's account of how natural settings produce rapid, affect-mediated decrements in autonomic arousal, independent of cognitive appraisal. Originators: Roger Ulrich · 1983 · Code: SRT On this page At a glance T1 parents References At a glance In plain English. Stress Reduction Theory (SRT) proposes that exposure to unthreatening natural settings triggers a rapid shift in autonomic and affective state — decreased sympathetic arousal, increased parasympathetic activity, and lowered negative affect — through evolved appraisal mechanisms that operate before and below conscious cognitive elaboration. The canonical evidence is Ulrich's (1984) Science study showing that post-surgical patients in rooms with tree-views recovered faster and used fewer analgesics than matched controls viewing a brick wall. pages.excerpt: Topic inspector — Knowledge Atlas complicated surfaces Complicated journey surface · Layer G · Structural inspectors Topic inspector Roles served researcher, admin, 160_student Current status naive Complexity tier Medium — per-topic reflective view Track 4 redesign target. The page exists at a placeholder level; redesign is open. Track 4 owns this page's redesign through seven graded stages: heuristic audit (T4.a), scenario walkthrough (T4.b), usability pilot (T4.c), severity rubric (T4.d), reproducibility check (T4.e), mid-fidelity redesign (T4.f), and final report (T4.g). Every track may read the page; only Track 4 proposes new designs. The full deliverable chain lives at Track 4 hub, Region 3.5 . ◧ What this page displays the information on offer Where the ontology inspector looks at the whole vocabulary, the topic inspector zooms into a single topic bundle and shows everything the Atlas has about it: the papers, the warrants, the interpretations, the mechanisms, the theories, the related bundles, and the extraction history. The inspector is the page a researcher lands on when they want to answer "what does the Atlas know about this exact topic, in full?" and the page an admin uses to audit a single bundle's quality — which papers belong, which mechanisms are implicated, whether the interpretation is stale. The page is tightly coupled to the main topic browser; the browser shows the same data at shallower depth. The inspector's job is to make visible what the browser hides

pages.title: Research Topics — Knowledge Atlas
headings: What does the research say about architecture and people?
pages.excerpt: Research Topics — Knowledge Atlas What does the research say about architecture and people? Knowledge Atlas indexes ~ 1,400 peer-reviewed studies on how the built environment affects human cognition, emotion, stress, attention, and well-being. We group those studies into topic bundles so you can see, for any question you care about, what the evidence actually supports and how strongly. Below, pick a topic the way that feels most natural. You can start from an outcome you care about (stress, focus, sleep), from an architectural feature you're curious about (daylight, acoustics, greenery), or see the full landscape. How we weigh evidence → ℹ︎ A few words you'll see on this page. A topic bundle groups studies that look at the same kind of environmental feature (e.g. luminous = lighting / daylight) and the same kind of outcome (e.g. cog.attention = effects on attention). The pill next to a topic tells you how settled the evidence is: defended means the evidence is strong enough that we'd cite it confidently; mixed means studies disagree or the sample is thin; unresolved means we can't yet align the term with a standard outcome label. Pick a starting point The page will ask one question at a time and only show you options that actually have studies behind them. You can back up at any step using the breadcrumb. If you'd rather see everything at once, there's a "Show me the full landscape" tile below. Looking for something else? → See a coverage map of where the evidence base is den

pages.title: Track 1: Image Tagger · COGS 160
pages.excerpt: Track 1: Image Tagger · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 1 TRACK 1 — IMAGE TAGGER Track 1: Image Tagger Build the visual annotation pipeline for the Knowledge Atlas — collect 500 images, write detectors for 6 latent tags, run them at scale, and ship a public viewer. 3 tasks 10 weeks 75 + 75 + 75 points What you’ll build The Knowledge Atlas studies how buildings shape the people inside them — how ceiling height changes thinking, how daylight shifts mood, how clutter raises stress. To study those effects computationally you need photographs, and you need each photograph to carry both a licence and a measurement. You will start with nothing and finish with a working pipeline. In Task 1 you gather 500 openly-licensed interior photos across fifteen room types, with full provenance for every file. In Task 2 you write six detectors that read those images and output latent tags — numerical scores for social-spatial features like sociopetal seating, prospect, and interactional visibility. In Task 3 you run the detectors over the whole collection and ship a standalone browser that lets anyone search the images by tag or by human effect. The collection, the detectors, and the viewer all become long-lived infrastructure for the Atlas. The three tasks TASK 1 Build an Image Collection Find five openly-licensed image sources, build a search pipeline, and gather 500 architectural-interior photogra

pages.title: Track 2: Article Finder · COGS 160
headings: Where harvested references live: the article_references table
pages.excerpt: Track 2: Article Finder · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 2 TRACK 2 — ARTICLE FINDER Track 2: Article Finder Build a PRISMA-compliant article discovery pipeline — fix the contribute page, extract knowledge gaps, generate targeted queries, execute searches, and triage results at the abstract level. 3 tasks 10 weeks 75 + 60 + 75 points +20 contract bonus on Tasks 2 & 3 What you’ll build The Knowledge Atlas needs a steady inflow of papers. Researchers working on environment-and-cognition need to know which questions the literature has answered, which it has only half-answered, and which it has not yet asked. Without a disciplined discovery pipeline, the Atlas drifts on whatever its contributors happen to have read this week. You will build that pipeline end-to-end, and you will build it to the PRISMA reporting standard — the gold-standard checklist for documenting how a literature search found, screened, and selected its papers. PRISMA is the difference between a search someone else can audit and a pile of links nobody can defend. You start in Task 1 with a broken contribute page that accepts PDFs and does nothing with them. You diagnose what is missing, write a contract that specifies the fix, hand the contract to your AI, and verify that submitted papers actually get classified, stored, and reported back to the user. In Task 2 you turn to discovery proper: you read the Article Eate

pages.title: Track 3: VR Studio · COGS 160
pages.excerpt: Track 3: VR Studio · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 3 TRACK 3 — VR STUDIO Track 3: VR Studio Build a pipeline that turns natural-language instructions into modified 3D rooms in real time — collect VR-ready interior models, prove every environmental factor can be manipulated, and ship an AI front-end that converts “a cozy library with 4-metre ceilings and exposed brick on the north wall” into an instant scene change. 3 tasks 10 weeks 75 + 75 + 75 points 120+ fps target What you’ll build The Knowledge Atlas catalogues the empirical evidence that interior environments alter human cognition and affect — ceiling height changes thinking, daylight shifts mood, materials change the feel of a room. A neuroarchitecture researcher who wants to test these claims has to vary one feature while holding everything else constant, which means she needs 3D rooms she can modify parametrically. No existing product gives her that. Tools like Veras and Decory produce 2D images, not editable 3D models; Meshy and Luma generate new models, not parametric edits of existing ones. You will close that gap. In Task 1 you assemble a library of at least 20 VR-ready 3D interior models, each annotated so a program knows which mesh is the ceiling, which is the north wall, which is a window. In Task 2 you load those models in a browser-based VR viewer and prove every factor can be modified in real time — sliders for

pages.title: Track 4 Overview · COGS 160
headings: What this track is and is not
The carried exemplar: the researcher persona, all the way through
pages.excerpt: Track 4 Overview · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 4 TRACK 4 · OVERVIEW GUI & Interactivity: Designing Evidential Journeys The previous tracks build the Atlas's content. Track 4 asks how that content reaches a reader. You will design and prototype an evidential journey : a sequence of pages that takes someone from a question they typed in to an answer that is properly warranted, qualified, and rebuttal-aware in the Toulmin sense — not a finished fact handed down from on high, but a defeasible conclusion the reader could in principle defeat by adducing further evidence. What this track is and is not This is not a usability-testing track. The Atlas is not yet deployed and there are no real users to recruit; the conventional HCI playbook does not apply. What we have instead is a richer, less common problem: design the journeys for personas whose questions, methods, and adequacy conditions vary widely, before any of those personas have actually used the system. The students in this track will spend three weeks (and possibly half of a fourth) producing the artefacts that make journey design possible: a tagged corpus of the questions a chosen persona would actually ask, a working catalog that pairs each question with a canonical answer shape and a defeater economy, and one journey prototype taken end-to-end as a demonstration of what the previous artefacts buy you. The track is heavy on

pages.excerpt: Task 1: Build an Image Collection · Track 1 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 1 / Task 1: Build an Image Collection Track 1 · Task 1 Build an Image Collection By the end of this task you will own 500 openly-licensed photographs of architectural interiors, organised by room type, with every image traceable back to the web page you found it on. Track: Image Tagger 75 points Contract gate The big picture The Knowledge Atlas studies how buildings shape people — how ceiling height changes thinking, how daylight shifts mood, how clutter raises stress. Studying these effects computationally takes thousands of photographs of real interior spaces. Your job is to gather 500 of them. But "gather" does not mean "drag images off Google." Every image must: Carry an open licence — Creative Commons, public domain, or equivalent. No copyright violations. Carry provenance — for every image you know where you found it, who took it, and what licence it carries. Carry a category — you know what kind of room it shows. You will build the tools that make this efficient, then use those tools to gather your 500. The 15 room types The collection must span a wide range of interior spaces. Use these 15 categories: # Room type What to search for Why we need it 1 living_room living room, lounge, sitting room Furniture layout, daylight, biophilia, colour warmth 2 bedroom bedroom, sleeping quarters Lighting warmt

pages.excerpt: Task 2: Build Latent Tag Detectors · Track 1 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 1 / Task 2: Build Latent Tag Detectors TRACK 1 · TASK 2 Build Latent Tag Detectors Write working Python detectors for six latent environmental tags. Each detector ships with a design note, a typed contract, and a test suite that runs against a gold-set of labelled images. 75 points Prerequisite: Task 1 Contract gate The big picture In Task 1 you gathered 500 architectural interior photographs with provenance. In Task 2 you tag them — not with simple labels like “living room” or “bedroom,” but with latent tags : numerical measures of environmental features that affect human cognition and behaviour. (Latent because the feature is not directly visible as a noun in the image; you compute it from geometric relationships among visible objects.) What the instructor already did (Sprint 1) Before this course began, the instructor finished Sprint 1: the 18 social-interaction latent variables (L41–L58) from the Environment-Cognition Taxonomy V2.6 (the project’s master vocabulary of environmental tags) are already integrated into the Tagging Contractor registry. That means: Every latent tag (L44 Sociopetal Seating, L17 Prospect, L42 Interactional Visibility, and the rest) already exists in the registry , with a full semantic contract. Each entry has a definition_long field that explains what the tag means in human

But you only tested them on a small gold-set. Task 3 runs them on the entire 500-image collection and then ships a viewer so humans can browse, search, and verify the results. The image-tagger repo already has an Explorer app (v3.4.74), but it is a full React/Vite application that needs a running backend, a database, and authentication. It is developer infrastructure — not something a student or researcher can open in a browser and use. pages.excerpt: Task 1: Fix the Contribute Page · Track 2 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 2 / Task 1: Fix the Contribute Page Track 2 · Task 1 Fix the Contribute Page The contribute page accepts PDFs but does nothing with them — no classification, no storage, no feedback. Your job is to repair it so submitted papers get classified, stored in the right place, and reported back to the user. Track: Article Finder Diagnosis · Spec · Fix · Validation Contract gate What this assignment teaches You will use AI to fix a broken page. The point, though, is not the fix — the AI will write the code. The point is the three skills around the AI: Can you diagnose what is broken by asking your AI the right questions? Can you specify the fix precisely enough that the AI builds the right thing? Can you verify that the fix actually works — and catch the silent failures the AI hides from you? Query design decides whether you find relevant papers or noise. pages.excerpt: Task 3: Search Execution & Triage · Track 2 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 2 / Task 3: Search Execution & Abstract Triage TRACK 2 · TASK 3 Search Execution & Abstract-First Triage Wire the four-scraper harvest layer (SerpAPI, scholarly , paper-scraper , and PDF-only-last-resort scidownl ), write every candidate into the article_references table, walk each row through the three-stage triage funnel (metadata-only, then abstract-and-classifier, then PDF retrieval), and acquire PDFs only for candidates that have cleared abstract triage — never before. The proof of the pipeline is a PRISMA funnel reconstructed directly from article_references . Track: Article Finder 75 points PRISMA deliverable Track: Article Finder · Prerequisite: Task 2 (you need your ranked gap list and query pairs). What you build: Run your search queries through SerpAPI (which scrapes Google Scholar), collect each paper's abstract through a fallback chain of free academic APIs, decide at the abstract level whether each paper is worth keeping, and report the whole funnel on a PRISMA-style dashboard. Core lesson: Never download a PDF to decide whether it is relevant. Triage at the cheapest level — abstracts — using free APIs. Then prove the pipeline works with real PRISMA funnel numbers. pages.excerpt: Task 1: Collect & Catalog 3D Models · Track 3 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 3 / Task 1: Collect & Catalog 3D Models TRACK 3 · TASK 1 Collect & Catalog VR-Ready 3D Interior Models Curate a library of at least 20 VR-ready 3D interior models, evaluate each against eight manipulation classes, segment the meshes so every wall is individually addressable, and produce a mesh_roles.json that the Task 3 AI front-end will consume. 75 points 4 phases No prerequisites The big picture The Knowledge Atlas catalogues evidence that specific environmental features alter human cognition and affect. To test these relationships in VR, a researcher needs 3D rooms where every factor — ceiling height, wall materials, lighting, window size, furniture density — can be varied independently while holding the others constant. Most 3D models on the open web cannot do this: they are single fused meshes, or they have baked lightmaps that cannot be swapped, or they are at arbitrary scale. Your job is to collect models that can. What makes a model "VR-ready" Five hard constraints separate models you can use from models you cannot. The first three are about how the model is structured; the last two are about file size and licence. Constraint What it means How to verify Separable named meshes The ceiling is a separate mesh from the walls; each wall is individually identifiable. Open in Blender → check the Outli

pages.excerpt: Task 2: VR Conversion & Factor Testing · Track 3 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 3 / Task 2: VR Conversion & Factor Testing TRACK 3 · TASK 2 VR Conversion & Manual Factor Testing Load at least ten of your models in a browser-based A-Frame viewer, build real-time slider controls for all eight manipulation classes, and produce a factor viability matrix supported by before/after screenshots. 75 points 3 phases Prerequisite: Task 1 120+ fps target The big picture In Task 1 you cataloged twenty-plus 3D models and annotated every mesh. Now you must prove those annotations are correct by actually loading the models in a browser and manually testing every manipulation. This is not a demo. It is validation engineering — and the matrix you produce is the input to Task 3’s validation gate. Why A-Frame, not Unity or Unreal Platform Learning curve Verdict A-Frame (WebXR) HTML + JS; loads glTF natively; runs in browser; zero install ✓ Use this Unity C#; 2-week onboarding; heavy project files ✕ Too slow for six weeks Unreal C++ / Blueprint; steepest curve; massive files ✕ Not feasible You already know HTML and JavaScript from Tracks 1 and 2. A-Frame wraps Three.js, a glTF loads with one HTML tag, and modifications are JavaScript property changes that execute at 120 fps in our prototype. pages.excerpt: Task 3: AI Front-End for Scene Modification · Track 3 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 3 / Task 3: AI Front-End for Scene Modification TRACK 3 · TASK 3 AI Front-End for Parametric Scene Modification Build a web-based tool where a researcher types a natural-language instruction like “a warm cozy library with 4-metre ceilings and exposed brick on the north wall” and the system modifies the 3D scene in real time, with every change validated against your factor viability matrix before it is applied. 75 points 5 phases Prerequisite: Tasks 1 & 2 The big picture In Task 2 you built sliders and swatches that modify 3D scenes in real time. But sliders require the user to know which parameter to change and what value to set. A researcher who wants to study “cozy versus clinical environments” should not need to manually set ceiling = 2.6 m, CCT = 2700 K, walls = wood, roughness = 0.6, lighting = 0.8. She should just say it. No existing product does this. Tools like Veras, DecorAI, and Decory produce 2D images, not modified 3D models. Meshy and Luma generate new models, not parametric edits of existing ones. You are building something novel — but every component uses proven technology. pages.title: Task 1: Build a Persona Question Corpus · Track 4 · COGS 160
headings: How to generate questions: three sources
pages.excerpt: Task 1: Build a Persona Question Corpus · Track 4 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 4 / Task 1 TRACK 4 · TASK 1 · WEEK 5 Build a Persona Question Corpus 75 points. Due Friday 1 May 2026 (end of week 5). The first move in designing for an audience is to know what the audience is asking. The corpus you build this week is what makes every later design decision in Track 4 testable. Why this task exists A great deal of design work goes wrong because the designer imagines a generic user with a generic question and engineers a generic answer; the generic answer satisfies no one. The corpus you build this week is the corrective. It forces you to commit, on paper, to specific questions phrased in the way your specific persona would phrase them, with the adequacy condition — what would make the answer feel insufficient — written out explicitly. Without an adequacy condition you cannot judge whether a journey is succeeding; you can only judge whether it is finishing. A second reason to begin here. Researchers, students, contributors, practitioners, theorists, and PIs do not ask the same questions. They do not even ask the same kinds of questions. Treating the Atlas as if it served a single user produces a system that is shaped to no actual user. Your corpus is the artefact that lets your team reason about persona variability concretely rather than as a slogan, because four students working o

pages.title: Task 2: Winnow & Fit Answer Shapes · Track 4 · COGS 160
headings: Read the researcher fitting catalog first
pages.excerpt: Task 2: Winnow & Fit Answer Shapes · Track 4 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 4 / Task 2 TRACK 4 · TASK 2 · WEEK 6 Winnow and Fit Answer Shapes 75 points. Due Friday 8 May 2026 (end of week 6). A corpus is necessary but not sufficient. The fitting move pairs every surviving question with a canonical answer shape and a defeater economy, and it is the move that makes a journey designable. Why this task exists Without commitment to what the answer to each question would look like , your corpus is a list. The fitting move — pairing a question with one of the five canonical answer shapes and naming at least one defeater the answer must surface — forces you to anticipate the journey before the journey is built. A page sequence that has not committed to an answer shape will wander; a journey that has not named a defeater will surprise its reader, and not in the helpful sense. The pedagogical goal of Task 2 is to move you from "questions exist in five flavours" to "answers come in five shapes, and pairing question to shape is itself a design move that can go right or wrong." It is intellectually the most demanding task of the three, because it is where you commit to claims about your persona's literature and where you have to find real defeaters in that literature rather than inventing them. pages.title: Task 3: Prototype an Evidential Journey · Track 4 · COGS 160
pages.excerpt: Task 3: Prototype an Evidential Journey · Track 4 · COGS 160 Knowledge Atlas Explore Evidence Gaps Articles Contribute Course 160 Syllabus Tracks STUDENT Home / 160 Student / Track 4 / Task 3 TRACK 4 · TASK 3 · WEEKS 7–8 Prototype an Evidential Journey 75 points. Due Friday 15 May 2026 (end of week 7), with a midpoint-of-week-8 extension to Wednesday 20 May 2026 if your team needs it. The first two tasks produced paper artefacts; this one produces a working one. A journey is not designed when it is described in prose — it is designed when a reader can click through it. Why this task exists The move from spec to prototype reveals everything that the spec elided. The prototype forces you to make choices the previous tasks could defer: which page does the reader land on; what is on the page above the fold; how is the rebuttal exposed; what is the next click. These choices are where journey design lives, and they cannot be made well without the corpus and the fitting catalog behind them. They also cannot be made well without actually building the artefact: a journey that exists only in description is a journey that has not been tested against the readers it was built for. The pedagogical goal is integrative. After three weeks of building intellectual scaffolding, you produce one working thing that demonstrates what the scaffolding buys.
